ASTOKIA. OKEGON:
FltlDA Y DECEMBER 18. 1SS5
DYXAMITEItS.
Sax Fj:anvisco is in a ferment
over the alleged discovery of dy
namiters connected with the anti
Chinese league in that city. San
Francisco is alwas ready to be
excited over something and feels
strange without a weekly sensa
tion of-some -kind-. The natural
tendency of San Francisco is' to ex
aggerate, to make things greater,
smaller, ghastlier, more glorious,
more horrible than they really are.
Hence all the talk must be tak
en with a grain of allowance.
Apart from this phase of the ques
tion there appears to be good rea
son to believe that something of
the kind has been discovered. In
a circumstantial way the statement
is made that the leadcrof the band
of dynamiters arrested is Henry
Weisman. He is a Bavarian by
birth, a chemist by profession, 25
years old, resided with his family
at 818 Pacific street, is president
of the German anti-coolie club,
and president of the Fifth ward
anti-coolie league. On being
questioned he said: "My inten
tion was to aid in enforcing what
O'Donnell told us was the law
ugainst the Chinese. The only
thing that troubled me was that
Coroner O'Donnell was always
saying that the Chinese were well
supplied with arms; that the' had
armories in Chinatown and were
drilling every night with "Win
chester rifles. 1 was afraid that
when the Chinese were ordered to
go they would resist and attack
our people. I therefore resolved
to experiment and see if I could
not invent a superior weapon. I
commenced experiments twelve
days ago and purchased some dy
namite. M..Y idea was to make
something that could be thrown
like a hand-grenade. We experi
mented at different times, under
the rocks near the Cliff house,
but the results were unsatisfac
tory. O'Donnell knew we were
experimenting. He had often
talked of dynamite, and he called
his newspaper the Anti-Coolie
Dynamiter"
"The man who gave the name of
Kooshier when arrested is also
known as Kewalski. He was a
Prussian, and speaks six languages.
He rented a small room on Lom
bard street. According to the
story, he was formerly a banker
at Kief, Russia, but was
driven from the country, and lost
all he had. He came to New
York, and was appointed an agent
of the Jewish relief society, and
visited Oregon and "Washington
territory to take up land for his
people. He was to receive $G00
for every family for whom he pro
cured a government settlement.
He claims that Henry Villard as
sisted him in his labors. He came
to this city and identified himself
with the anti-coolie league.'
"Charles M. Meladstadt is presi
dent of the Third ward anti-coolie
club, and vice president of the
German branch of the same. He
is a German by birth, 24. years old,
and resides at 40i Geary street.
In bis room the detective - found
300 badges, bearing the letters,
'D.A.C.S.'"
"Oscar Eggers lives at S12 Clem
entina street, and is a peddler bv
occupation. He is 35 years old,
and speaks very little English."
Commenting on the matter the
Post says: "San Francisco is af
flicted witli demagogues. The
half fool, half rogue is a dangerous
mixture, and San Francisco is
well acquainted with the type.
For some months past Coroner
O'Donnell, a perfect specimen of
the class, has been enjoying him
self and worrying the city more or
less. His election to the office of
coroner last fall was a very poor
joke, which has brought its own
punishment, for it turned O'Don
nell's head and inspired him
with the crazy ambition to be
governor. In order to keep him
self before the public hp has been
delivering Snnday harangues on
the sand lot. He has made the
Chinese question his hobby. Not
long ago he issued a proclamation
commanding the Chinese to leave
the city before January loth next,
or take the consequences. By
talking largely of hidden arms and
deeds of valor and blood to come,
he has produced a more serious
result than he calculated upon or
wished, precisely as at San .lose,
last summer, he impelled by his
extravagant sp'eeches, a poor wit
less wretch to set fire to the prop
erty of a dozen citizens guilty of the
crime of employing Chinese labor.
The police have discovered a dy
namite conspiracy, and the conspir
ators are men who have attended
O'Donnell's meetings, believed
what he said, and set about carry
ing his bloodthirsty vaporings into
deadly effect. Among the men
allied with the coroner in his cru
sade against the Chinese, and for
office, are a number of crack
brained socialists, always ilad to
help along any agitation that
promises a violent outcome. As
for the dynamite conspiracy
which the police have exposed,
we are not inclined to be
lieve that it is nearly so serious
as the fears of some and the in
terests of others wish the public
to believe. The people of San
Francisco are pretty hard-headed,
and not devoid of a sense of humor.
The general inclination has been
to smile at O'Donnell's sanguinary
speeches and his military prepara
tions. Nothing is harder for a
crank to bear than to be laughed
at and it is highly probable that
the chief object of the bomb
makers who have been arrested
was to make some sort of demon
stration which would startle and
frighten the city into taking them
seriously. The police are to be
commended for their faithfulness,
and it is to be hoped that the mur
derous fools who have been ap
prehended will be dealt with as
severely as the law will permit.
As for O'Donnell, this should be
a warning to him. The people are
patient, but there is a point de
yond which they will not permit
disturbers of this kind to go. He
has reached that point."
It is unfortunate for the Pacific
coast, whose dwellers really want
to see the Chinese so, that a lot of
orazy cranks have taken up the
cry in San Francisco and else
where just for the sake of notori
ety, and bring the movement into
disrepute. Every time they make
a move they make a mess of it
and retard legislation just so
much.
Ex- Speaker Ra.xdall is firm
in his opposition to the attempted
change in the rules of the House
of Representatives. These rules
are more important than would at
first appear. One of the results
of a change of procedure would be
to take from the committee on ap
propriations direct charge of
several of the appropriation bills.
Randall says that that change
alone would cost the treasury
200,000,000 before the close of
Mr. Cleveland's term of office, or
about 50,000,000 per year.
Ml
At English elections the gov
ernment prints the tickets, a custom
which to Americans ap Dears
singular. The tickets are prob
ably "straight." At the last elec
tion in this city less than 800 votes
were polled, but at least 25,000
tickets were furnished
Ml
Gen. James R. O'Beikne says
in the New York Star that the
Irish national league proposes to
pay a stated salary of $1,500 to
Parnell and the eighty-three na
tionalists recently elected to the
British parliament.

YtWIKOS "T-X 3M oUUulGllGl I'liiF the Hawaiian Gazette, March'' 164 1881,
ir
Hopiy
Of thB Minister of the Interior to
the Memorial on East Indian
Immigration.
Depaktmext op Interior,
Honolulu, Mar. 9, 1881.
Messrs. Geo. C. Williams, J. Wight, E.
Bond, D. .B. Vida, and others.
Gextleuek : In the acknowledg
ment of the receipt of the memorial
you tlid mo the honor to send me, I
promised you an eariy repij ; circumstances
intervened -which caused my
time and attention to be so fully en
grossed that I have been obliged to I
delay my response, and I trust you i
will accept my apology forihe delay.
In your memorial yon call my attention
to the deficiency And cost of the
labor 6ugply of the country, and al
lude to tne lact than in other sugar
producing countries East Indians -were
employed, and state that you believe
the introduction of these people "would
be productive of great ana permanent
benefit to this community.
In considering the subject of the
supply of labor for the country, it is
the belief of the present government
that the matter of labor should "bo regarded
as subsidiary to the larger and
more important national Question of
population ; that, so far, it has been
the general policy of the government
t5"secure for the planters such ad vantages
that tlio wages of the laboring
cl&jfsesvwoolcl be snehas to stimulate
iSe native population to industry and
enterprise, and further to invite the
immigration of men -with families, who
would odd to the permanent population
ofthe country, rather than to seek simply
for the cheapest supply of laborers
without reference to the higher needs
of the country. It was hoped that the
exceptional advantages of the reciprocity
treaty with the United States,
even for eight years, would put the
industrial interests of the country upon
such a footing that they would be able
to maintain themselves even if that
treatv was not continued in operation
afier that term.
I see no reason to anticipate the abrogation
of the treaty, or to doubt the
afiility of the country to compete with
other sugar producing countries if it
honld be terminated.
Ton call my attention to the
by colonies wKich are
able to obtain the services of East Indian
coolies for a term of years at apparently
low rates of wages, and in effect
ask why steps are not taken to secure
the tame benefits for this country.
This is no new question, but is one
srhiohiLas received very careful -consideration.
Perhaps the best answer
is, because it has never been shown
that either the cause of population or
cheap labor would be served by the
introduction of the class of East Indian
who leaves India as a coolie laborer;
on the contrary, those "who
have considered the subject on "behalf
of the government have arrived at
quite the opposite conclusion, and the
xperimentatFiji which was watched
Great interest seems to justify
those conciusieBS : although it may be
possible that in Jlainysis. or India
there may be lound people from vhom
a class of immigrants could be found
who would recruit the population of
thexHJuntry, they would hardly be froni
th'e clas who emigrate to sugar grow-In
countries as coolies. The investi
gation of this subject, however, so far
as population is concerned, is now in
the lianas 01 a xioyui uoauuiBaiuuur,
and the government await with interest
hit report.
Begardmg the subject of labor by
itself, the importance of which I have
no dtsire to underrate, I am of the
opinion that the advantages enjoyed by
UOlouies emjjiuj'iug ttBUiiuwu ,""""
have been overrated; even if the
coolies could be induced, to come to so
distant and to them unknown country
as this to labor under five years contracts
in the usual form, at a shilling a
dav which is the minimum rate at
wnivh he India government allows
them to bo xecraitea, wo must add to
Ifiis the cost of passage to this country,
with interest, and of returning
Uwm to India at the expiration of their
oontract; the cost will then be nearer
tan Hollars rer month than the six
mention. The cheapness of
labor does not, as you are aware, de
pend whollv, on the rate 01 wages, dm.
gh tho Quality and quantity of the
'PSxipGB
i. fin for as I have
been able to gather reliable information,
the East India coolie compares
very unfavorably with either the
Chinese or Polynesian,
t In a report of H. B. IL's Commissioners
of Enquiry into the condition
and treatment of immigrants in JJriUsu
GmaBS, nhensome millions of dollars
and many1 years have been spent
in establishing the East India coohe
svsteor the opinion is decidedly m
fivor of the superiority of the Chinese
Sjver the East Indians as laborers, and
regret is expressed that the Chinese
Immigration should be termmated.
Another writer on British Guiana
soealani: of the Chinese, states that
&jTa "more saber and reliable
ihan any other laboring cUss in the
cdosy j and previous to!866 Chinese
coolies were introduced into Bombay
itself. On this subject of wages an
authoritative work published in
says,
Tide Prize Essays on Agricultural
Subjects, printed by order of the Royal
Agricultural Society of British Guiana,
Page 90: As to wages, to have already
mentioned that a shilling a day
is the minimum sum that the Guiana
government on behalf of the planter
guarantees the immigrant for his work.
But tho latter cannot bo forced to work
during all the hours prescribed by law
for this fixed sum. The law presumes
that by working six hours in the field
or seven in the building a man will
have fairly earned hiB BhiflinEr, and di
rects that he shall receive it. Bnt the
planter is bound to pay Mm at least as
much as ho pays nnindentured laborers
for the same description of work,
sodas labor is scarce, the rate of pay
isliigh. A good field laborer can,
without oyer exertion, earn two shillings
a day at all descriptions of work,
and can command a higher price at
those which require a litUe extra skill
or experience. It is no exaggeration
to say that an able bodied agricultural
laborer can, if he works hard with his
Bhovel for six hours during five days
in the week, cam from two-and-a-half
to four dollars, (ten to sixteen
Oh the sarnsuTyect, in the TJeport
of Her tMajeltyJe -Colonial" Land and
Emigration Commissions for 1876,
kindly furnished me by Sir Stephen
"Wblcott, C. IT. G., one of tho Commissioners,
IJind it stated as one of
the principles governing Indian
are lobe the same
as paid to unin&entured laborers on the
same estate; and in the same report
they say, referring to the "West Indies,
vuxe uoionizauon uircmar, issuea
by Her Majesty's Colonial land and
Emigration Commissions, 1877, Page
547 : " Agricultural laborers almost
invariably work by the task, the usual
pay for which varies according to cir?
cumstances from one shilling to three
Ehillimrs per day. An able-bodied and
industrious laborer can earn from two
to three shillings (75 cents) per day,
and these amounts are actually earned
by Creoles of African descent and by
the more powerful Chinese and East
Indian immigrants."
Of the quality of tho laborer, I have
never been, satisfied that it was equal
to what this country has been accustomed
to, or would adapt itself readily
to our system. The most favorable
estimate I have ever received regarding
the East Indian coolie was that
three of them were equal to two
Chinese or Africans, and that it required
special tact and patience to
bring them even to this standard. In
the essays to which I have alluded it is
saiu,
Vide Prize Essays, Page 62 : "The
disinclination for work is so strong in
these men as to induce them to prac
tice alternatives which are simply in-
creaiDie to J&uropeans. jnany win
keep painful sores open for weeks in
order to prolong their stay in the Hospital
: as soon as they are cured and
discharged they will start Another by
means of a rusty nail or something of
that kind. Some will purposely eat
food especially prohibited by the doctor,
to ensure prolonged enjoyment of
the Hospital, although it be accompanied
by a loathsome and painful illness.
The East Indian mind, or to
speak more correctly, the mind of that
class of East Indians which furnishes
immigrants, has,for ages looked upon
poverty, disease and dirt as marks of
high caste piety, and regards the giving
of alms to persons practicing these
disagreeable habits "as a most meritorious
action."
Again :
Vide Prize Essays, Page U : "The
Hindoo, who in his own country is
the most sober of men, becomes in
British Guiana infected with the prevailing
love of rum, and may be seen
reeling about, yelling in bis frenzied
excitement, or lyinglike a log by the
roadside in a state of drunken stupor.
The debauch of Sunday is followed by
its natural reaction, so that on Monday
the unhappy votary of drink is most
unwilling to exert himself. On the
other hand there are some well-conducted
immigrants, who make a rule
of fasting every Sunday, and owing to
this unnatural abstention from food the
devotee is often on Monday as unfit for
work as his debauched brother.''
Again :
Vide Prize Essays, Page 92 : " One
of the worst enemies Jo the coolie is
the village lawyer; the native of India
has a natural love of litigation and a
readiness to complain about trifles."
Again :
Vide Prize Essays, Page 58 : "The
Asiatics never seem to be content with
leaving a case to its own merits ; it
appears to them necessary to embellish
it with a quantity of collateral
falsehoods," &c.
Again :
" The East Indian, not trainedup in
early life fo endure the hardships of
toil, not accustomed to rely upon his
own individual exertions or to work
his path.through life by his own self-reliance
and -self-denial, is. quite un-
: UJSUtm i. J 8
, ji
suitedvto be cast on his own resources
and left to take care oF himself in this
colony."
These quotations I give because they
accord with the information given me
by several pardeswho eniployedthem
in tho TVest Indies, and who without
exception told me that Cape do Yerd
Islanders and Chinese were greatly
superior asjaborers j and also because
they explain tne necessity of a code of
laws which must in their operation be
very vexations to planters, in order to
secure that protection to tho Indian
which tho Indian Government insists
upon as a pre-requisite to consenting
id me emigration, ana wnicn toe imperial
Government charges itself with
seeing carried out.
I am aware that it has been sometimes
asserted that the oversight of
Great Britain is a mere formality, and
that these people are upon a footing of
British subjects generally, but when
in London I found no such opinion
prevalent, or any desire to make it appear
that the necessary conventions or
arrangements were meaningless,' or
that ordinary treaty rights were sufficient.
It was clearly stated that the
coolies were considered as wards of the
Government, and that special protection
was intended to be given them.
In this the East Tndian and Imperial,
Uovernments are probably only
a duty which experience has
shown the necessity of. In dealing
with foreign countries the custom is to
demand the passage of certain laws,
and stipulate that they shall not bo. altered
to the prejudice of the coolie
during the term of his service, a regulation
which would require class legislation,
or curtail the exercise of domestic
legislation over the whole range
of laws affecting the relations of masters
and servants. This "would probably
be readily acceded to in a case
where the greater part of the laboring
population were .hast Indian coolies,
but should hardly be seriously considered
in a self-governing, independent
state, where the larger part of its
own citizens were -of the laboring class.
On this subject of special protection
I may quote the language of Her Majesty's
Commissioners :
Vide Colonization Circular, 1877 :
"They are protected by special lais
and by special officers appointed to administer
those laws."
It should be remembered that the
whole coolie system as it is called,
grew out of an exceptional state of affairs,
and is still, more tolerated than
approved by a largo class of British
statesmen, and may at any timo bo abruptly
terminated.
The fact that special arrangements
have to be mado with Great Britain
before any of theso people are allowed
to emigrate, is sufficient to show that
East Indian coolies are not held to be
in the same position as any other
British subjects. Theso special arrangements
cover the minutest details con
cerning wages, food, lodging, hospital
accommodation, &c, as long as he remains
abroad, and provide for his passage
homo, and ho is not free to make
any other kind of contract of emigration
as any other British subject is.
It may not be inopportune to recall
here the circumstances under which
the system of transporting laborers
from the British East Indies to the sugar
growing colonies of Great Britain,
under contracts for long terms, arose,
and to refer at the same time to tho
principles upon which the system is
conducted.
It is now forty-seven years ago that
Great Britain, under tho pressure of
advancing popular sentiment, compelled
tho British Colonial sugar planters
to abandon the Bystem of negro
slavery, a system under which they
insisted it was alone possible to continue
the profitable production of sugar,
and therefore to continue its production
at all ; and when the forced apprenticeship
system of tho negro, which
followed tho abolition of slavery came
to an end, it was found as a matter of
fact that large numbers of sugar estates
went out of cultivation and had
to be abandoned. The British Government,
seeing the difficulty and anxious
to conciliate and assist the Colonists,
organized with tho consent and
assistance of the East Indian Government,
what is known as the East Indian
Coolio, system, by which Indian
laborers, earning very low wages in
their own coon try, were induced to make
contracts at higher wngos to serve as laborers
for long terms in the British sugar
growing colonies. The Colonial Governments
became responsible in each caso for
the selection, transport, core, treatment and
return of these people, and stringent rules
were insisted upon by tho British Government
in order that no injustice- ehonld he
practiced upon the ignorant and passive
.East Indian when removed from his own
country. Notwithstanding these precautions,
complaints were from.timo to time
presented to the English people, principally
through meetings held at Exeter Hall, London,
that the Indians were ill-used, and
.that the .system was only a modified form
of slavery; tho government was urged to
abolish it, and it was stopped in 18d8 for
some four years ; it was recommenced under
still more stringent regulations for the
protection of the East Indian, as it was
on all hands that he was hardly

capable of taking care of himself. It is
now believed that in no British Colony has
tbeEast Indian any just cause of complaint,
but it is only becausoho is held as a
ward of the British Government, whilst
the laws of the Great Empire of India and
more particularly of each Colony are
to be specially modified to suit tho
system. In these appendages to tho British
Crown, aboriginal races havo no voice?
profitable sugar growing is the one grand
object, and a plentiful supply of cheap labor
is of coin-so the great means to that
end, so that they frame their laws as bell
they can in tho attempt to try and meet the
philanthropic views of tho people Of Great
Britain and at the same time enable them
to control the coolie labor. It has been remarked,
perhaps with more of sarcasm
than justice, that the form, of government
of those Colonies is " un autocracy or sugar
tempered by Exeter Hall," an imputation
however under which any colony-or
country that relies mainly on raising sugar
by East Indian contract coolie labor is apt
to rest.
Great Britain has extended this privilege
of employing East Indians to certain Colonies
of Fxance, Denmark and Holland, on
condition of passing and maintaining in
force codes of laws approved by the Indian
Government.
It is of importance to bear in mind in
considering the working of this Bystem,
that the importation of a certain limited
number of coolies usually supplies the demand,
but that as the old contracts run out
and the coolio goes back to India with his
accumulations, or finds out another method
of living ia the Colony, new lots of men
from India have to be obtained to keep
down the rate of wages to its normal level.
The working of the system in the neighboring
island of Mauritius7, an island of less
area than Hani, has resulted in a somewhat
overcrowded population on January
1st, 1880, of 357,339 .souls, of which the
Indian population-numbered 243,586. Of
these 150,857 were males and 92,529 were
females, showing an excess of males
of 58,328. Tho excess of males
in the whole population taken together was
at the 6am o date Gi,5 01, showing that the excess
of males was mainlycaosed by the coolio
system, ana which is remarkable in an isl
and so close to India, and where tne com
munication is so cheap and frequent that
the Indian Government does not insist on
the coolies being granted a free passage
back to India, as they do in nil other Colonies
to which they aro permitted to emigrate.
The agents of somo of tho Colonies
admit that it is next to impossible to obtain,
the prescribed proportion of Indian women,
even by " sweeping tho streets" of tho
towns of the most abandoned class of females.
It will bo seen therefore that when tho
people, tho government and the planters of
this country commit themselves to this system,
Great Britain consenting, they do not
simply admit a few or even a considerable
number of laborers or people to be added
to the general supply, bnt rather they inaugurate
a labor system which seems usually
to swamp all others, at least if intended to
accomplish tho object which you propose,
that is to seenro a steady supply of cheap
labor obtainable without reference to tho
suitability of tho peoplo as a permanent
population for the country.
With tho views held by this government
aB to the general impolicy of adopting the
British India Coolie system, it may perhaps
be unnecessary to refer here in much
detail to the difficulties which stand in the
way of satisfactorily arranging the matter
with tho British and Indian Governments.
Having however been requested whon in
England to look into this natter, it may bo
well to summarize the results of my enquiries
at head quarters. In the first place,
it is absolutely necessary before introducing
a single Indian under contract, either
to make a special convention with Great
Britain, or to agree to bring them under
the terms of some convention already made
with another country or colony. In carrying
out such an arrangement it would be
necessary for this k'ngdom to modify its
laws to suit the system. The Imperial
Government insists on certain strict regulations
for tho protection of the coolie, but
at tho same time the Colonies find it necessary
to make some very stringent laws for
their control, which I am convinced wonld
bo pronounced here unconstitutional, for
the protection of tho planters and to enablo
them to get a fait amount of workfrom
the coolie, who is admitted on all hands to
bo " indolent to a fault" unless held under
some compulsory law to work.
Where the population consists mainly of
East Indian coolies, or similar people, there
is usually not much difficulty in passing
such laws ; bnt situated as we are with an
intelligent native, foreign-born and mixed
laboring population, who have tho selection
of the law-makers in their own hands, the
difficulties would seem insuperable ; nor is
the difficulty any less if it is proposed to
modify the Constitution so as to permit
class legislation, and apply laws to the
coolies which wonld not affect the general
population. Even in British Guiana, with
a largo Indian population, the system of
making laws to suit other countries is
found to bo inconvenient, and it is staled
in tho essays previously alluded to,
yide Fslzo Essays, -Pago 59 : "A great
misfortuno to the Government of Guiana,
as indeed must be expected in any country
the laws of which are passed at the dictation
of other countries, is that very strin
gent laws not always well digested are
sometimes crude." s
The British officials in London reminded
me that in trying to obtain East Indian
coolies for this country, we should have 'to
encounter the passive opposition of tho
East Indian Government, which quite
agreed with Dr. Hillebrand's Bcporfc to the

namely, tho difficulty wo might have
in kcenintr nn'n snnnlv of East Indian

My own observations on the subject of
the introduction of an East Indianlaboring
class, were that economically it wonld not
be advantageous to tho planting interest,
as I think tho labor would be dearcrTncost
than either Chinese or Portuguese labor is
likely to be, and moro difficult of management
; that socially, all the evils attributed
to the influx of Chinese Would be still further
aggravated by the East Indians, and
new evils growing out of their, caste prejudices
and Bociar habits, wonld necessitate
new and perhaps conflicting laws as well
as a staff of interpreters and legal officers
for their special care and protection. That
politically it would be inexpedient, consider
ing the lack of clearly defined advantages,
for a small and weak government like this
to complicate its relations with Great
Powers by tho conclusion of special conven
tions regarding the details of municipal
legislation concerning lanonng ciass aueu
in character and habits, suspicious and litigious,
whoso petty complaints might bo
constantly involving their -employers and
the country, asthey have other countries, in
long, irritating, diplomatic disputes.
In conclusion gentlemen, let me say, that
xiis .Majesty s government recognize tne
great importance of tho industrial interests
you represent, and tho important relations
iQt'j oear to ue 01 iQO wnoie
population of tho kingdom, native and
foreign. They are well awaro of tho importance
of the careful adjustment of the
cost of production in tho world's markota,
but they aro in hopes that tho question of
labor pure and simple will soon solve itself ;
and I think the period which has elapsed
since I bad tho honor of receiving your
memorial has shown that we are not Without
sonrccs of supply of cheap labor, and
that such supply is likely to be of such an
extent as to rather cause somo anxiety lest
the demand bo moro than filled and a surplus
be left in regard to which somo gravo
qnestions may arise.
I am, gentlemen, with great respect,
your obedient servant,
H. A. P. Cabteb.

August 10.?British Ciulana is about to
come face to faco with a serious labor
problem, the result of u decision made
by t!ie British government discontinu
ing- the indenture system, by which
moans thousands of lOast Indian coolies
are now brought to this colony each !
year to work on the plantations. The
employers of this kind of labor are j
given live years' grace in which to ad
just themselves to the conditions that
will have to l>e met.
The prosperity and progress of Brit
ish Guiana to-day are due in no little
measure to the presence of these
?oulie.s who are th ? backbone of labor
in the island, unci who enable the plan
tation owners to grow sugar ar.d roco
profitably. The wages of these blast
Indians are lower than any pay re
ceived by other luborlng classes in the
western part of the world, and. with
perhaps one or two exceptions, are far
1* ss than any workers in the world are
paid. Without these coolirs the plant
ers of British Guiana will find the cost
of production mounting rapidly, for
the negroes of the island will not work
for the low wages given to these In
dians. So far in the history of this
colony it has been necessary for th<>
employers to have cheap labor in order
to get satisfactory returns o.i their in
vestments. If the prices on sugar and
euro should drop .?o.isicerahly with the
ending of the indenture, the planters
would be hard pressed to grow and
market these products at a reasonable
profit.

The coolie as a laborer is a blessing
to British Guiana, though it is very
questionable whether the system which
brings him here should lie permitted
to continue. Opponents of the coolie
Immigration plan, who, through their
influence, have induced the government
to decide against it for the future, term
it slavery; and perhaps it is. in a mild
meaning of the word. The land pro
prietors and others who have profited
through coolie services defend the in
denture with arguments that the East I
Indian is better off here than In
crowded India, and that his labor is
t.ecessary to make cultivation profit-i
able. Both sides have points in their
favor.
It is estimated dial there now arc
more than 200,000 Kast Indians in this
colony. During the early years of the
indenture system many thousands of
tliese laborers were brought here with
their wives. Kapidty they multiplied,
and the coolie colony soon became a
large one. It has continued to grow
fast from year to year, and is still
increasing in spite of the war. which
has denied the planters full opportunity
to take advantage of their years of
tiYaco. So it is ?asy to see how im
portunt tlu*s?* Indians are in a country
which depends upon them for labor, and
especially upon they are .1 full third
of a total population of 300,000 people.

In 1S3!> the Itritish government really
began the importation of coolies. At
that time it appointed special agents
at Calcutta to superintend and regu
late the exportation of all able-bodied
Kast Indians who should wish to cross
the seas for work. L'nder the inden
ture plan thus inaugurated and still
continuing, the coolies are bound to
work for a period of five years on
plantations to which they are assigned.
They are fully protected, of course, by
government rules, which compass the
immigrants as soon as they reach the]
colony. At this part health officers in
spect the Indians and give them treat-|
ment to insure that they will land in
licorKetcuvn sound and well. The j
health of the coolies is guarded
throughout the period of the inden
ture t oo.
Ue'or" the coolies are. placed on the |
plantations they are insured goo:l )
treatment through contracts which
their employers are compelled to m.tks
with the government. In tl"c acrec
ment the employers consent to provide
clothing, food, mediea! services, suit-j
able living quarters and otherwise look
afier the welfare of the laborer?. The j
clothing and food clauses now, how
ever. are a part of only a few of the j
contracts, since the wages have been
raised to pcrnvlt the coolies to clothe!
hemselves and provide their own food.!
1m making the allotments to the various
I'l.Mi:ations the government refuses to :
permit families to be separated, and
in event of in. rrixge a coolie woman
is allowed to follow her husband to his
place of employment.
The governtne it is ever watchful to
see that the indentured Indian is well
treated. It has always reserved the
right to transfer the coolies from anv
planter who tlofs not take good care
of them to .mother employer who
promises to do much b? tier. Of course,
the government also protrr-ts the plant

era against coolies who refuse to work
or obey the laws that sire laid "Duwn
for them. The employers aro permitted
to Hue the recalcitrant or wayward
coolie whenever negligence or por
verseness becomes his master, .tabilual
absence from work has up until the
present year been punishable by Im
prisonment. The coolie who refuses
to work has been regarded as a de
serter, and has been constantly dealt
with in a severe manner.-?
Such strict regulations \s those
which permit the planters to imprison
the Indians or to line them heavily
have Klven the opponents of the Inden
ture system grounds for some of their
best arguments against the practice
of bringing the coolies from India un
der the present plan. They were chief
among the arguments advanced in the
recent hearings that brought about the
decision to discontinue Indenture.
Since the government took this drastic
step the planters and other persons of
influence have been working publicly
to better the conditions of the Indians,
giving them wider range for pleasure
;inil making more favorable terms. The
imprisonment for labor offenses has
l>een abolished; lines have been greatl?
reduced, and payment of these assess
ments has been made easy through the
installment plan. This course of action
has been adopted in hopes' that the
British authorities will recognize, the
,-hanges being made for the better, and
ivill rescind their drastic action, or at
east make modifications that x. ill not
lenv the planters all the advantages of
he Indenture system.
Consideration also has been given to
.lie advancing cost of living, for high
lrices of food have affected even these
Oast Indians. In the early days of
he indenture, the coolies received only
:S cents a day. This wage has been
ncreased until now tlift average carti
ngs of the coolie man is 30 cents a day
ind of the woman is cents. In April.
317. a typical Hindu estimated the
ost of living, including food. oil. wood
in 1 tobacco for himself and wife (yes.
i.ost of tIto women smoke i. uui bis live
hlldrcn?four and one-half adults?at
15.36 a week, or IS cents a day per
'lull. As man is estimated to need
hree working suits, two ordinary suit"
ml two hats a year. A woman requires
our suits. The expense of this cloth
ng is $16,24. or about 1 cents a day.
Ths cost of living then to the average
man ami wife la 40 cents a ilay, leav
ing a surplus jm the week of S cents.

British Guiana, like Trinidad, .pays
a heavy toll In the way o? a long
criminal record for having within Its i
boundaries so much cheap k.bor. The ?
negroes in all the British possessions!
of this part of the world are a source j
of much trouble to the colonial govern- !
j menl because of their tights. thefts, j
: disturbances and numerous odd crimes', j
I but here In this South American colony ;
| tlie presence of the Kast Indian in- j
I creases the law violations to a very
I high number, and gives the police much !
| to contend with at all times.
j The average East Indian is no men- j
ace to tlie peace and safely of the white j
j man. It is among his own race that !
! he is a very bad fellow if he happens j
j to be crossed. The coolie invariably '
| wants to settle all differences himself J
j without waiting the hand of the law. '
j lie wishes to be his own Judge and I
| executive, and therefore ho is much in '
I court, if a coolie is seriously offended j
j by another of his race he is very apt]
j to satisfy himself by killing the of- j
| fending man?or woman. Or if lie j
fancies he lias been wronged in some j
trade or in some dealing. he either will )
wage a fist fight or immediately so to .
law with lils trouble. Lawyers, there
fore. find the coolie a very good source
of revenue. During t.he sessions of
court the scenes around the house o!
justice in Georgetown are often very
liirturcsque, for at tills time all the
.?oolies and their families come to
(own. The women are dressed in tin*
.jayest of colors and wear all their
adornments.
Many Kast Indians are hanged in
British Guiana and Trinidad for mur
der. Among the coolies it is an un
written Iaw that death must come to
a wife who commits a cardinal sin.
A woman is a slave to her master, and
she must obey him iii every way. if
she refuses, he more than likely will
disfigure her face; if she betrays him
she is sure to die. provided lie learns
ot her dishonor: and among these Kast
Indians news of this aort travels
mysteriously and fast. Ari Kast Indian
woman who asserts herself or who
seems to have an air of Independence
is very rare?so rare. Indeed, thai one
may wander through the coollo villages
Tor weeks without mcotli such a
woman.
Hut even with all these peculiar peo
ple?and the situations that arise from
their presence?British (Juiana is a
Well-Koverned colony. The law is
.Htriotly enforced throuKnotit the entire
country. It Is administered quickly
iind fairly, and seldom does a guilty j
utan escape. The Constitution under!
which British CSuiana is governed Is '
different from any Kast Indian colon v. i
The ollicers of the government ar>; {
comprised in a governor, an executive ;
council, a court of policy and a com
bined court, the last consisting of the |
governor and members of the court,of i
policy and six financial representatives.)
Tho mombers. of tho court of policy,
whoso duties arc i>ow purely legisla
tive. used to l>o elected by a college of
electors, but thoy are now chosen by
:i direct vote of the people. Enforce
ment <>f the police laws Is vested In a
police inspector, who is an olllcer of
the British iinny. The present In
spector has given the colony a very
strong and commendable pollco or
ganization. probably the best In Its his
tory. The military band attached to
this department Is considered tho finest
ci-U-nit-l band in the British possessions
of this part of the world.

a
Washington, Sept. 1.—The Chinese
government .proposes to take a. hand
in the question whether the Panama
canal is to be built with Chinese coolie
labor. At least that government is to
see that laborers are not supplied for.
this purpose unless they are paid a
wage scale which the Chinese author
ities regard as adequate.
What such a wag® scale will be it
s too early to say, but an official of
the Chinese legation, ventured the
prediction that it would not b« less
than $1 a day.
When this fact come officially to the
president and the Panama canal com
mission, the canal labor problem may
take on a new aspect. In fact, it may
have to be abandoned altogether, for
it is the cheapness of the Coolie labor
as much as its efficiency -which has
recommended it to our government.
But all estimates made of coolie
labor are based on what is paid else
where or what would be sufficient
compensation to induce the coolie to
come, if he were left by his govern
ment to make the choice himself.
Thirty Cents.
In many parts of the world the Chi
nese coolie works for 30 to 50 cents a
day, silver. The idea of paying the
coolie more than $1 a day will prob
ably strike the commission as prepos
terous.
The United States government is
not in a fortunate position to argue
the matter with the Chinese authori
ties. Our course in excluding coolie
laborers from the United States for
the past twenty or thirty years stops
us in a way from complaining if the

New York, Sept. 1.—There is a thea
tre In this city which regularly, day
after day, attracts audiences made up
of learned men who come fropi the
four corners of the globe. You will
not And Its name listed in the theatre
bulletin at any hotel, yet its fame is
worldwide. The theatre is always
crowded whenever a performance is
on. for, as a rule, each drama is dif
ferent from the preceding one and
each is realistic beyond anything ever
portrayed by the masters of dramatic
art. The chief performer is invariably
a distinguished artist, and it is in his
work that the interest of the audience
centers. Oftentimes his wonderful skill
and scarcely less wonderful self-con
trol—an all-important feature of his
art—result in what might truly be
termed a miracle play.
For the theatre is the celebrated one
connected with the post graduate
medical school and hospital. The lead
ng actor is some skilled and courage
ous surgeon. The audience is com
posed of doctors, old and young, na
tives and foreigners, all gathered for
the single purpose of perfecting their
knowledge of the science of saving hu
man lives. The subject of the. drama
is usually some patient such as the
medical fraternity in this country ac
knowledges can be found, excepting
now and then, only In the metropolis
for here in New York are millions of
all sorts and conditions of men. Here
are quarters each with its own dis
tinctive army of foreigners—an Italian
quarter as large as a seaport on the
Mediterranean, a Jewish quarter like
another Kishineff, a Greek quarter,
hardly less impressive than Athens it
self and in these various quarters dis
ease takes on curious forms. The
post graduate hospital, where special
attention Is paid to the most difficult
and baffling cases, is Consequently a
sort of composite institution sugges
tive of all the European centers and
that is what makes the school the
mecca of advanced students ot medi
cine and surgery.
Of this school the most conspicuous
feature is the operating theatre. As
In an ordinary theatre, work on the
stage begins before the audience Is
seated. First of all, an orderly washes
the marble walls of the pit and the
tiled floor of the stage Itself, for the
scene of the drama about to be enacted
must be as speckless as human hands
can make it. Then the nurse who plays
the role of sponge-wringer—a quiet,
prepossessing young woman, attired in
blue, whose particular duty it is to
look out /or the sponges and tampons
—appears on the stage. For the space
of five minutes, she does nothing but
wash her hands and her arms and then
for three minutes more she immerses
them in a solution of bichloride of
mercury. After that she is prepared
to play her part. Her arms and hands
are absolutely free from germs and
she must take care not to touch any
unsterilized object until the perform
ance is over.
Next the assistant nurse takes three
big white pitchers and plunges them
in a germicidal bath and then fills
them with microbe-killing solutions in
which from time to time the surgeon
will dip his fingers. She also places an
antiseptic cloth upon the table at which
she is to stand, and in a tray she lays
a sterilized napkin to hold dressings,
sponges and tampons. It is part of her
duty to keep tally on these things as
she hands them to the surgeon. Over
the frame of her table, and over that
of the table which the surgeons Is to
use, she stretches a white sterilized
ho.d for the purpose of protecting the
dressings and the instruments from
any chance microbe that may descend
from ,the gallery.
The next person to arrive on the
scene is the head nurse. Her duties
preliminary to the opening of thf
drama consist chiefly in selecting from
a cabinet behind the scenes, which be
longs to the surgeon Who is soon to
appear, the Instruments to be used
during the operation. These are ster
ilized, wrapped In a sterilized napkin
Hard Bargain
Chinese government takes a Kand in
excluding them from our canal strip
when we want to bring them here.
The Chinese government can well
say: Our laborers have not been'good
enough for your country all these
years now if you want them yoq^will
have to pay to get them. .».
It is not impossible tftat the Chinese
government may make use of our ne
cessities in the matter of canal dig
ging to insist* on a relaxation of the
Chinese exclusion laws. This Is the
first time China has had a chance to
drive a trade with the United States.
It is the first time the celestials have
held any cards in the diplomatic game.
Needs Little Food.
A coolie is the technical WOrd used
in tltie orient for any day laborer. It
is derived from words which indicate
that he works for a daily wage. Most
laundrymen are coolies.
The coolie laborer is ordinarily a
lean, shambling, hollow-thighed indi
vidual, with phenomenal powers of en
durance. He can work more hours on
less food than any type of laborer in
the world. He work3 patiently and
systematically, and never with excite
ment. He lives when at home on
dried fish, rice, seaweed and similar
simple foods.
He does jot drink alcoholic bever
ages, and his only dissipations are apt
to be small gambling and a moderate
use of opium. Many coolies will work
twelve and fifteen hours a day.
The canal commission, in employing
coolie laborer, will be compelled to
make contracts with each individual
coolie, although the laborers wlH come
to the* United States in charge of a
general contractor.

and placed in a sterilized tray.
Now it is almost time for the drama
to open, and the audience begins to
gather.* Some of those in attendance
come from a clinic or lecture and oth
ers fram a demonstration in one of the
wa-rds of the hospital. They take their
seats with a solemnity that befits the
occasion. Once the doors are ogene«l,
the seats are quickly filled. The por
tion of the theatre set apart for the
audience Is semi-circular. The rows of
seats incline quite sharply ffoa, a
height a little above the stage
At last the star performer enters,and
as he crosses the stage there is
slight mumur which correspond^ to
the applause of the audience in an or
•linary theatre. For a moment he re
tires behind the scenes to nave his
hands sterilized and his costume put
on. When he reappears he is wearing
what looks like a priest's robe and a
bit of headgear which, many-colored
as a Scotchman's bonnet", might in
other circumstances, be described as
dashing.
Now all is ready for the performance.
The door at the side of the stage open
ing in the direction of the hospital is
thrown back and the subject of the
drama is wheeled in unconscious and
placed upon the table near which the
picturesquely garbed surgeon is stand
ing. With that each spectator in the
seats above leans forward in his anxie
ty to catch every word and every mo
tion of the chief actor. Then cames
the prologue. The surgeon recites the
history of the trouble that laid the
patient low, and theii outline^ his
course of action.
After Ihis prologue the surgeon be
gins the work of restoring the sick per
son to health. The miracle play is on.
Everything moves with wonderful
quickness and^ precision. The strain
on the audience is Intense in fact, it
seems that some of the spectators are
in danger of falling over the railing
so eager are they to see what is go
ing on. Presently all lean back and
give a. sigh of relief. The performance
—and the strain—are over. The mir
acle has been worked,. The subject is
still unconscious, but he will awake in
the sweet, white bed in the hospital to
know that in a short time he will be
as a new man. On all sides may be
heard expressions of congratulation
Few theatres in the world are the
scenes of so many successful and im
portant performances as this one.
Meantime, the hero, as he might be
called, retires again behind the scenes
to remove his robe and bonnet and
wash hjs hands in the antiseptic solu
tion the head nurse removes his won
der-working instruments and the
sponge-wringer disposes of the re
maining dressings. In a few minutes
the theatre is empty, but the stage is
soon ready for the next performance.

Crosby, N. £.. Sept. 1.—A battle
royal between the Soo and Great Nor
thern companies over the Crosby town
site impends. Both roads are building
into this section, the Soo a branch
from Flaxton on its main line and the
Great Northern a branch from Berth
old, also on its main line but neither
will enter Crosby's present site, which
has for two years been the trade center
of northern Williams county, it being
contemplated that the grade is such
as will not permit of the location
sidetracks.
The Soo platted a new Crosby toWn
site two and one-half miles northeast
of the present location and put the lots
on sale, but Crosby business men re
fused to buy, for many reasoha.,aU
of whi^i they made known to the Soo,
except the chief one—a hankering be
lief that the Great Northern would plat
townsite only a mile or two to the
south, and, hemmed In between the
international boundary line and the
Great Northern road, the Soo town
could never be a good trade center.
Whether unconscious or not of this
feeling on the part of the people, the
Soo,
with a heart to please, platted a
new townsite nearer and free from the
fornter's objectionable features but
the sale of lots proved a frosty ac
knowledgement of the road's generous
disposition, onlj* a few sales* it is said,
having been made.
All Doubts Dispelled.
The Soo people contended that the
Great Northern would not build into
Crosby this year, but the latter, while
not presenting evidence as tangible
as desired by many has fortified itself
strongly with the business men
of the town by verbal representations
and such evidence as it was possible
for the road to put forth at this stage
of construction. All doubt that has
existed as to the company's sincerity
has been dispelled now, however, as
about 500 men and teams arrived here
last week and grading is beihg rapidly
pushed from this end.
The following want ads of eath road,
which have been appearing in the twin
city dailies, will give an idea of the
.sharpness of the contest:
Wanted Merchants to Know That
Splendid opportunities can be found
for the establishment of stores of All
kinds in Crosby, the promising new
town in Williams county, North Da
kota. Track will be laid to Crosby
next week big crops now being har
vested and must be marketed there.
C. A. Campbell, Townsite Agent, Soo
Line, Minneapolis, Minn.
Wanted Merchants and Others to
Know*" the exact situation at Crosby,
N. D. do not be misled by clever ad
vertising all ordinary lines of business
well represented in Crosby at the pres
ent time. See Land Commissioner
Great Northern railway, -St. Paul,
Minn., for locations in new. towns
The Soo line runs West from Flaxton*
about siJt miles south of the interna
tional boundary line to Ambrose, a:
point about nine mllea west of Crosby,
and the Great Northern, from near
Flaxton parallels the Soo, about a mile
and a half to the south, and its termlt
rial will be one mile east to the present
Crosby. The business men of Crosby
have decided to cast their lot with the
Great Northern, and for this reasoit
all ordinary lines of trade "will be wey
represented in Crosby on the Great
Northeni.
it is probable the Soo will have t»

is but little doubt that the postofficg
will go with the rest of the business
houses.

The Great Air "Whale.
The native inhabitants of the Fiji
Islands believe thunder to be the bel
lowing of the great air whale, a myth
ical creature which, according to their
folklore stories, inhabits the opposite
side of all thunder clouds. If the regu
lation thunder cloud appears unaccom
panied by the ominous rumblings pe
culiar to such phenomena, they take it
as a token that the Great Spirit is
pleased with their past doing. If, how
ever, on the other hand, the detonations
be frequent and violent they are sure
that the god of the elements is dis
pleased and immediately engage them
selves in all sorts of ludicrous incanta
tions for the purpose of righting lhat
ters. Lightning is to them the result
of the air whale gnashing his teeth to
gether and thus striking fire. Another
peculiarity of this primitive people lies
in the fact that they will not partake
of food while a thunder cloud Is in
sight, fearing that the air whale will
pounce upon and devour them for such
irreverence.

Speech and Intellect.
inan who has, by disease or injury,
lost the faculty of talking is generally
also unable to write, and it is only in
exceptional cases that one of these
functions persists while the other is in
abeyance. Cases of this latter kind
show, however, that there are really
two separate centers for the two fac
ulties
are lying very close to­
gether and therefore generally suffer
at the same time. If the disease af
fecting them be still more extensive
the faculty of intelligent pantomime or
gesticulation is likewise abolished. Per
sons who have entirely lost their lan
guage may still be able to play chess,
backgammon and whist, and they have
been observed to cheat at cards with
some ingenuity. They may also be
eharp in business matters, facts tend
ing to show that speech and intellect
do not run in identical grooves.
The Struargrlee of a Sculptor.
Augustin Rodin, the French sculptor,
had a great struggle with poverty and
adverse criticism in his. youth. Of an
episode of this early period a writer
says: "With that inflexible will and
singleness of purpose that never failed
him throughout his career the young
sculptor set himself to express iu clay,
marble or bronze his conception of life
and art. He used his leisure in work
ing at nighttime, in a deserted stable
he had transformed into a studio, at
his wonderful mask of 'The Man With
the Broken Nose.' After eighteen
months of hard and patient labor he
finished this mask and sent it to the
salon. It -took the hanging committee
fourteen years tq. .discover awy mei it
In the work, for it was rejected in 1804
and finally accepted only in 1878.'!

text:
THE BLUB BOMB
BY J. V. OIESY
" A Story Of Humor, Mystery, Romance, and Adventure 99
He came in siphfof the beach ami
of a trim launch, into which several
men seemed lifting an impassive fig
ure. With a yell he leaped forward
and charged down upon the group.
Ono of them, standing a little apart,
turned toward him. A dart of flame
leaped out. and Gafford heard the
whine of the bullet as it sped past.
He lifted his own weapon and fired
as he ran. His panting breath and
his motion caused his shot to go
wild. In the few instants since he
had seen them, they had placed the
burden they carried in the launch.
Now the man who had fired at his
appearance leaped aboard. The
launch moved forward, gained head
way, and swept from the shore.
Galford reached the water's edge
and paused breathless before the
strip of widening water. Uy just
that much he was again too late.
Oshitu had made good his escape.
From impulse rather than purpose,
the man on the beach lifted his re
volver and fired again after the
'speeding launch. In a way it was a
defiance, a declaration of war to the
last.
A moment later he began to re
trace his steps. He had but one
thought now to get back to Kate
and report his pitiful failure, and
plan how he might still continue the
pursuit. "God help her! God help
her!" he panted as he ran across the
island to where he had left the sam
pan. Surely the woman who had
gone an unwilling captive on the
launch of Oshitu had need of help.
He found his man waiting. For a
moment he had feared that even he
might have deserted. But no doubt
he, too, had heard the pant of the
launch's escape, and decided to wait
for his passenger and collect his
pay. Gall'ord boarded the sampan
in silence, and courtly ordered the
man back to the water-front.
A man of a lesser fiber would
have been totally cast down. Gaf
ford, on the other hand, seemed to
himself to be turning to ice. The
continued success which attended his
enemy's efforts was rousing a deadly
cold purpose of combat within him
which nothing else could have done.
The light, aside from all question
of nations, was narrowing to a per
sonal issue. This man had begun by
wrecking his life prospects, soiling
his name, driving him into exile, and
to the very brink of the oblivion of
the lost. Now once more he was
flaunting his every effort. Tonight
he had won by the narrow margin
of a strip of water but he had won.
Deep in his soul, Galford swore that
the next time he would not win.
From now on he would take up
the trail, and not pause until he had
fun him down. He had a triple pur
pose to urge him; the life of a na
tion, the honor of a pure woman,
and his personal score. Each foot
that the sampan crept back across
the moon-silvered water seemed to
put some of the steely reflection of
the glinting rays into his soul. His
face took on new lines of firmness
while he sat and waited for his ar
rival at the beach.
The white yacht lay sleeping, as
they passed her. Not a soul aboard
her dreamed that the girl who had
come as her honored guest to this
port had been abducted under their
very eyes. Gafford scanned her
closely, reading the lettered "Nan
tucket" upon her pointed bows. And
it seemed to him that at that instant
a new link in the chain of his en
deavors was forged by Fate. He
nodded slightly in approval of the
thought which had waked, and be
gan to plan anew.
Gradually the sampan moved up
the harbor. The little fire of the
boatmen winked from the beach.
After a time they reached it, and
Gafford rprang ashore. Without a
glance he handed the astonished
boatman a piece of gold. How much
it was or how little he did not notice
as he turned away.
The man burst into frantic avow
als of undying gratitude and cut
the m suddenly short. The man ho
addressed was already racing along
the beach in the direction of the
quay.
"They are an odd people, these
Americans," mused the boatman,
"and 1 am certainly lucky beyond all
my hopes. Here in my hand I hold
much rice and fish, and the price of
a fowl or two. Ai! Th Gods are
good."
GnfTord in the mean time had
reached the first street of shops, and
still running, arrived at the House
of Moon Face.s. . He entered and
made his way back through the tea
rooms, spied White Kate as he
passed, and signed her to follow.
Once in the seclusion of her rooms
he turned upon her with a white
face and lips that snarled.
"They have beaten us, Kate," he
said harshly. "They got the girl. I
was too late:"
CHAPTER VII.
A Self-Possessed Coolie.
White Kate's lids half closed for
a second. Her hand, half lifted to
her bosom, clenched.
"Where, have you been then?" she
questioned quickly. "Gaffy you
haven't"
Gafford laughed roughly.
"No, I haven't!" he snapped in an
swer. "I've lccn too bu.-y, and I've
been a little bit of all over. I took a
boat-ride on the bay to pass the
time."
Kate bit her lips. "Forgive me,"
she requested. "You were gone such
a long time, and I have been anxious.
What do you mean by that stuff
about the bay?"
"Sit down. We've got to talk,"
said Gafford. "Give me a cigarette."
She handed him one from a pack
age in the sleeve of her kimono, and
he lit it while she dropped to a seat.
"It's just as I told you," he went
on. "I was too late. I always have
been too late in my dealings with
Oshitu; but, by the Lord, I'll catch
up with him yet! That's what I want
to talk with you about. I'm on his
trail. I'll find him and his bombs
and this girl. Think of her, Kate,
kidnaped and dragged off by that
yellow devil! Think what her fate
must be." He ground his teeth in
an access of rage and loathing dis
gust. '
"I am thinking of her," said White
Kate in a strange tone of voice. It
seemed, indeed, as if she might be
thinking of something or some one
I far away. "Well, what will you do
next? Have you formed any plan?
"Follow him, Kate. Just follow
him. Follow them both until I get
Oshitu, and destroy his menace to
our country and save that girl.
You're sure your information about
the steward on the yacht was cor
rect?" "I think so, Gaffy. What hap
pened tonight would go to prove it."
"Right. Now, another" question:
could that geisha find out anything
about where Oshitu is buried while
he makes these bombs?"
"Not directly. Still, it may be
that one remark will serve," said
Kate. "She heard him tell Yamata
to address him under the name of
Hashimoto at Kobe. That would
look as though he were somewhere
in that region."
Gafford nodded. ''And it would
be made comment. Somewhere back
of Kobe they could tuck a plant
away in the hills. Kate, I think I
had better go to Kobe tonight."
White Kate widened her eyes.
"Go to Kobe tonight? How?" she
gasped.
"In the Nantucket McRae's
yacht," Gafford replied.
Kate smiled.. "They'd be likely to
take you without McRae's orders or
permission. You yourself said he
was in Tokyo. Oshitu knew that
when he chose his time to strike."
"I've an idea they'll take me all
the same," said Gaflord; "and there's
something you've got to do. I want
to be disguised."
"As what?" inquired Kate.
"As a Jap. If I'm going poking
around the neighborhood of Kobe on
Oshitu's trail 1 can't go as a white
man, can I? You've got to make me
up. You haven't forgotten the art
since you left the stage, have you?"
White Kate shook her head. "I
see," she said. "You'll want a coolie
suit and not much else, except to
have your skin painted. All right,
Gaffy; I still have my make-up out
fit. I'll get it. Strip yourself to the
waist."
Gafford nodded, rose and threw off
his jacket and shirt. Meanwhile,
White Kate knelt at a trunk and rum
maged out an old stage make-up box,
which she brought back to a stand
and pushed to Gafford's side. She
rolled back the sleeves of her kimo
no, preparatory to mixing a stain
which should darken his skin; and
suddenly Gaflord cried out:
"Kate! What have you done to
your arms?"
White Kate gave him a glance and
a smile. "Nothing," she said.
"Hut those bruises! You're black
and blue to the shoulders! It looks
as though some one had struck and
maltreated you. What happened?
Who did it? I want to know?
"I'm glad you don't know." She
smiled her response. "I didn't think
you did but I'm glad. I've more of
the things on my body, Gaffy. You
tried to get out of the room the other
day, and you nearly made it. I
called for help, and it came just in
time."
Gafford's eyes dilated in shocked
horror. "You mean I did that
attacked you made those marks?
Kate!" He clenched his hands and
turned to pace the length of the room
ami back to her side. When he spoke
again his voice was tense with emo
tion. "I'm sorry, Kate more orry
than you can know. God! I must
have sunk to the beast level to have
done a thing like that. I never lifted
my hand to a woman, yellow or
brown or white, before, no matter
what happened. I didn't think I ever
would."
He put out a tentative finger and
touched her arm. "Are they painful?
Hut of course they are."
"Never mind it, boy," said Lhe
woman. "It wasn't you, Gaffy it
was the thing we were both
fightirg. You didn't know what you
were doing and I don't mind the
bruises. They were won in a good
cause. Oh, boy, I'm so glad to sec
you conquer! Don't say a word more
about it. Come here and let me make
you up."
He seated himself without a word,
and she set to work sponging his skin
with a brown stain. IJy and by he
told her of the events of the evening
the trip to the Nippon, the pursuit
to the island, and the escape, of Oshi
tu with the girl.
"I suppose he'll run straight out
side," he surmised, "and transfer her
to a vessel or a coasting banka, or
they may even run un the coast in
the launch. They could do it. Any
way, I figure that Oshitu will take
her with him, and Oshitu is evidently
going to Kobe. The girl has spirit,
and he won't find her an easy victim.
It took leveral of them to put her
in the launch, and from the way they
handled her I Imagine she was
bound."
"Poor kid!" said White Kate as he
paused.
She began working on hia face, fin
ished, and dropped down and rolled
up the legs of his trousers: tinted his
feet, and legs from the knees, and
Copyright, by th Fraak A. Muotey Company
rose. She picked up a hand-glass
and held it to him.
"Take a look at yourself," she in
vited. "Thank Heaven, Gaffy, your
hair is straight and black and your
eyes brown. I think your hair had
better be cut 'short."
Gafford looked at his changed self,
while she plied a pair of scissors and
a comb. He grinned at the new vis
age which looked back at him.
"You might add that the high
cheek bones are an asset, too," he ob
served as he laid the glass down.
"How long will this dye stuff last?"
"About three days if you don't
wash," said Kate. "I'll give you a
bottle of stain to take along."
Half an hour later he was ready.
To all seeming he was nothing more
than a coolie. Dozens of his fellows
could be picked up any day on the
waterfront or be seen playing their
parts of bearers, drawers of rick
shaws, rowers of sampans, beasts of
burden on the wharves and lighters.
Unlike them, he carried Yamata's re
volver and a supply of cartridges, a
small mirror, a strong knife, a bot
tle of stain, and a sponge, to which
Kate now added a small chamois bag
with a long string.
"What is it?" asked Gafford. "A
charm?"
"The most potent of charms," she
retorted. "It will open doors, pris
ons, secrets, and other things. It's
money, boy."
"How much?"
"A thousand, Gaffy."
"Dollars?"
"Yes."
A slow flush mounted in Gafford's
cheeks under the stain, and he put
down the bag.
"I can't take it, Kate," he protest
ed. "I already owe you too much,
anyway, both of money and other
things. A man can't take money
from a woman and keep his self-respect."
"Rut how are you going to get
along without it?" she argued. "How
are -you going to Kobe? Oh, don't
be silly, Gaffy! Take the rotten stuff
and turn it to some good."
"I'll get to Kobe all right," he as
sured her. "I've got a scheme."
"Rut I'm not giving it to you," she
persisted. "I'm giving it to the fight
for our country. You'll need funds to
put this thing through. You're doing
the work, running the risk.?, endan
gering your life. Won't you let me
do my part? You don't mean to
shut me out? It's my country as
much as yours, Gaffy, and it doesn't
matter what I am or what I've done.
I love it, Gaffy I love it as a person
does something he can't ever have
any more. Please, Gaffy " she ex
tended the bag toward him "please
boy. My part, Gaffy my part."
"Dut I don't need it. I can get
money to nut this over, I tell you.
I'm sure or it, Kate. Why, McRae'd
put uo ten times what I'll need when
he knows. I've an idea you've been
saving that to go back to a white
man's world with some time. You've
snoken of such things to me before
this. 1 can't take that money from
you, girl. I"
White Kate set her lips in pur
pose. Her face quivered for an in
stiOt and then she spoke:
"We're wasting precious time. You
haven't time to get word to -McRae,
let alone get money from him, Gaffy.
I told you once that perhaps I'd tell
you what it was that brought me to
this place why 1 killed that man.
I'm going to tell you now. Listen.
"In those days when you first knew
me back home I had a sister. She
was younger than 1. Virgie we called
her; her name was Virginia. She
was just a living sunshine in those
days. And because there are men
who can't see a beautiful thing with
out wanting to possets and crush it,
and because Virgie was a child in
her faith in a promise, though a
woman in years, the sunshine of her
life went out. When it was too late
she came to me and told me all about
it. It was one night in my dressing
room, after the show, 'there isn't
any use telling about-' that scene. 1
took ray little crushed baby sister in
my arms and promised to stapd by
her. That night she died, Gatfy
by her own hand.
"From that time on I lived for re
venge. She had told me the name of
the man who had wronged her, and I
made it a point to get into touch with
him. I threw myself in his way, and
after a time he took notice. I outdid
all my acting in trying to fascinate
him. and all the time I loathed him,
hated him, meant to kill him when I
could. Finally 1 yielded to his plead
ing and agreed to meet him in a cer
tain place.
"I met him, Gaffy, and I told him
about my sister, and then I killed
him, while he groveled on the floor
like a dog and begged me not to
shoot. They didn't hnd him for two
days, and by then I was gone. I left
the country, and I've drifted to this.
Now, do you understand?
"That girl who was stolen tonight
is the same sort of girl as my sister
young, sweet, full of life and am
bition and hope. She is in deadly,
peril in the hands of a man whom
we know to b a brute. Can you
think what it means to her what it
may mean to her? Gaffy, I want you
to save her before it is too late, as
I would have saved my sister had I
known in, time. I want you to take
this money and go save her-from
what threatens.
' "I'm not giving you this money, or
lending it to you, on anything like
that. I'm giving it as a pledge for
her life and honor. Take it and go.
Take it asa pledge from one woman
for another, from the one who would
like to be as simple as the other is.
Please, boy, understand and take it
and go. Please, Gaffy, please!" She
paused and began to tremble. "Oh,
for God's sake, let me be worthy of
the name of 'woman just once!" she
cried out.
"Kate!" said Gafford. "Kate!
Don't 1 I never knew how you felt; I
never dreamed. Here; give me . the
stuff. I'll take it and I'll save that
girl with it. You're white a white
woman, Kate the real thing white.
I'm going now, old girl; wish me
luck."
She seized his hand and wrung it.
"God go with you, boy," she said
with a sob. "There won't be a minute
of the time till I hear but I shall be
thinking of you. Goodby, Gaffy, and
luck."
A dim shape slipped into the alley
back of the tea-house of Moon Faces.
It slunk along in the shadows as
though seeking their protection. Its
bare feet made no sound as it shuf
fled over the uneven passage. It
reached the end of the alley and
turned along the street which led to
the quay.
Some time after a sleepy sampan
coolie, lying outstretched on the sand
was surprised beyond measure to
open his eyes and discover what ap
peared to be another of his ilk bend
ing above him.
He started up, and his surprise
grew. The seeming coolie placed a
round piece of gold in his hand and
whispered. To the wakened one's
sense of hearing it seemed that he
said: "For Nippon."
Before he could collect his wits
further the figure had left him,
stalked to his sampan, shoved it into
the water, and leaped av;oard. Ere
he could scramble to his feet and cry
a protest the figure in the boat had
seated itself and begun to row.
The coolie's eyes came back to the
gold in his hand. He lifted his fin
gers and scratched his head. "Two
gold pieces in one night!" he ex
claimed to himself and sat down on
the sand. "Ai! what a night indeed!
Ai! My luck has changed!" He lost
interest in all else and sat counting
his gold. The figure in the sampan
rowed on.
It seemed in truth to be a night of
surprises. The officer of the Nan
tucket's deck in the first watch waa
surprised to see what appeared to
be1 the head of a native appear abovo
the forward rail just after four bells
had gone. It was followed by the
arms, shoulders, and body of the
man, who came over and dropped to
the deck.
Mr. Mathison, second officer,
deemed it about time to take a hand.
He sped forward and reached the fig
ure, where it was just scrambling to
its feet.
"Here!" he rasped sharply in good
United States. "What in time are you
trying to do? Get off this boat!"
Immediately he received his second
surprise. The coolie finished the pro
cess of rising, surveyed him calmly,
and addressed him in perfectly good
English: "I really can't do it, old
fellow. There's a considerable tide
running, and the sampan I came in
is by now somewhere between here
and the China Sea. You wouldn't
ask a man to sim a tide rip, now
would you?"
Mathison's jaw dropped and then
closed with a snap. "Who the devil
are you?" he demanded. "What are
you coming aboard like this for?
What do you want?"
"To begin with," said the coolie,
"I am as white as you are. I came
aboard by your stay chains because
the accommodation ladder wasn't
down, and I want to see your sailing
master or captain right away."
"If that's all so, why didn't you
hail us?" Mathison was suspicious.
The coolie grinned. "It requires a
harsher spirit, Mr. Officer, to throw a
man off after he has boarded than to
keep him from getting on," he ad
vised. "Still, we're wasting time,
rather. My mission is important, and
I would suggest that you notify your
superior of my presence. Just tell
him how 1 got here and say that my
errand concerns Mr. McRae."
Mathison started. "McRae?" he
repeated in evident surprise. "Here,
you come with me. We'll get you to
a light and see what you look like,
anyway."
He led the unexpected guest back
to the rear of the deck-house, opened
a door, and escorted him to the after
saloon, beckoned one of the watch to
stand guard outside the door, and
then turned and looked his compan
ion up and down. He was a young,
well-set-up fellow, whose face now
wore a puzzled frownN "You may bo
white. You sure speak like a white
man. What are you secret service?"
he asked.
"I'll explain to your captain," said
the coolie, and sank into a chair.
Mathison frowned again, more
deeply, turned, and disappeared from
the room. He wa3 back again in -a
few moments and signed the coolie
to follow. "Come into the captain's
cabin," he directed, and led the way.

A delegation of six English cotton
manufacturers Is now on Its way to ;
this country to make a three week’s
tour of the American cotton industry.
The visitors are members of the Man
agers’ and Overlookers' association of
Bolton. England, and are making the
trip under the ausr»{ces of Lord Lever
hutme, Mayor of Bolton.
The delegation is especially inter
ested in technical education and labor
orfraoUution and its members have
planned a careful study of those two
.subjects ns well as the methods of
rnanufadluring and merchandising in
the cotton industry.
The visitors are scheduled to arrive
Id AVw Vork about September 12 and
will first visit tin* spinning and weav
ing center^ of New England. Later
they are to he taken through the in
dustrial centers of the Southern states.
Elaborate plans for their entertain
ment have been made * by a reception
committee comprising Homo of the
most prominent cotton manufacturers
ami financiers in tee United Statea,

Coolies Keep House
in Shanghai on Wage
of Four Cents a Day
Shanghai, Pepf. 13. —A31 nations
havp i n e n who sell their strengths—
who have nothing else to sell where
by to make a living except physical
strength, which Mn be'turned to ac
count in unskilled labor—but China
is the one nation above all others
‘he majority of whose men folks have
no other way of making a living
They re the coolies, whose only
friend ■ appears to be the Americans
who ary sent here by churches in the
T'nited States.
Th— word “coolie" means “the man
who fells his strength." The coolie*
are found in many classes of labor,
but the ricksha coolie is the most pic
turesque to the Orldenta! mind and
the -m.; ‘hat lingers longest in tie
memory or anyone who has visited
China or Japan. The ricksha is not
indigenous to !h» Celestial republic,
hut was imported from Japan about
half a century ago
rn this city alone there are about
10.0AO public rickshas with a shifting
population of probably many times
that number of ricksha coolies Epis
copal church workers estimate that
the total number of coolies ill bhang
ha! . including all who "Sell their
strength” in physical labor of all
kinds, is about 400.000, or two-thirds
of the city’s population.
Th.1 Ilf* of trio ricksha roeUc is
brief -from ihroo to five years, ton at
most. Heart disease, induced by their
Croat physical exertion, and, tuber
culosis. which is China's "yehew
plague." carry off the majortty. Most
of those taken to the mission hospi
tals for treatment are Jn a state of
ecdians* iiue to inauthc feot food, bad
living conditions and the intense phy
sical strain caused by running.
A ricksha coolie earns, on an aver
a*re. three or four rents a day. or
about as much as an American work
man would earn in ten minutes or
less T’pon this pay the coolie must
support himself and his family In a
town known throughout China for its
high cost of livine Those who $re
married live in little colonies in the
city's outskirts. They pay from fif
teen to twenty cents a month rent for
the small straw or bamboo hots In
which they live.
In the winter the entire family
eratvls into the one Httle room and
huddles about the charcoal ilre where
the rieevis conking. in order t^i keep
warm * Many of the babies contract
'eye disease from the fire, and tph^cco
smoke, which later ends In complete
f blindness. The unmarried rlchsha
boolie sleeps wherever he can find
shelter—most often in the cheap tea
i houses, where forty or fifty stretch
ion the floor of one room,
f It is anions the coolie class that
the mission workers are now laboring
1 with special real. The coolies at first
‘ could' not understand why anyone
ishould want to do anything for them,
accustomed as they are to centuries
inf neglect and abuse, hut after the
' first mission was established especial
ly for the coolie class they eagerly
I received the spiritual and bodily aid
! ottered them.

Manufacturers of imitation' uncut

Several of the largo Jobbers and
