Silk Underwear
Is No Longer a
Woman’s Wear Luxury
_
Silk underwear has ceased tirrne re- j
garded by a majority of. American
women as an article of luxury. The
demand for has become so general
that. In the opinion of the manufac
turers. It Is looked tlpon as a neces
sary staple in the feminine wardrobe.
Never before in the annals of the in
dustry has so much silk under wear
been worn by women. One large
manufacturnr recently stated that
snmnTbr sales of his product were 190
per cent greater than for any oth< r
corresponding period.
The output for fall delivery was
entirely consumed three months after
It was placed on the market and So
heavy is the demand for the spring
lines that manufacturers are prepar
ing to distribute productions on an
allotment basis. Merchants Lrorn the
smallest and most sparcely settled
communities of the T'nlted Ktates are
writing to local manufacturers ask
ing for terms on silk underwear.
Retailers throughout the country
report that customers repress a de
cided preference for the high priced
garments. Prices that wiil obtain
this spring will average about Jit 8.i
for vp.sts and $3.50 for bloomers.
The demand is virtually confined to
women’s garments. Men do not gen
erally favor that class of under wear,
and because of the tremendous press
of business manufacturers are not,
as a whole, pushing the sale of men's
lines.
Woollen Goods Exports.
Exports of woollen manufacturers
from this country during the seven
months ended July 31, 131?. were twice
the value of the exports for the simi
lar period last year. The woollen ex
ports for the first 7 months this year
w»re valued at $32 79^X3*. against
$11,759,172 for the first seven months
of last year, and $10.<16!.502 for the
same period in 1917.

VOL. XXI. No. 16
Washington.—In a speech in the
senate favoring an investigation of
the recent marine strike, Mr. La Fol
lette said:'
"I lay down this proposition that
American seamen are essential to the
American merchant marine.
"I start this discussion with the
proposition, which will not be contro
verted by anyone at all familiar with
the subject, that we can never have
an American merchant marine unless
we can attract to the marine service
and retain in it genuine American
seamen and officers to man and oper
ate the ships. I am not going to take
the time to elaborate that proposi
tion. Not only does the history of
every maritime power in the world
prove its truth, but the manifest pur
pose of every country in the world
today except the United States, with
any maritime pretensions, to man its
merchant ships with its own citizens
shows how universally the truth of
the proposition I have just stated is
recognized by the maritime nations of
the world.
For a hundred years we have had
the money we have had the busi
ness we could easily have had the
ships. The reason we did not have
a merchant marine was because we
had not attracted American seamen
to the service."
The speaker proved that the strike
was not over wages, but over working
conditions. Admiral Benson, then
head of the shipping board, who stood
with the ship owners, is quoted be
fore a senate committee on May 9
last:
"I think that the question of wages
is always a little exaggerated because
under any system it is not much
over 10 per cent of the total cost of
operation."
This means, said Senator La Fol
lette, that out of every $1,000 for
ship operation, $100 went to labor,
and the reduction which the shipping
board demanded and agreed to Ijy the
men, is $15.
The speaker said that the mistakes
of an unskilled or inefficient crew
would more than offset this so-called
wage saving in a single voyage.
"It is not a question of wages at
all. It is a question of destroying the
men's organizations and of subjecting
them to working conditions which
they will not stand, and ultimately of
driving the American seamen and the
American merchant marine from the
sea, enabling the ship owners to em
ploy cheaper foreign labor and make
still larger profits.
"These men, when they could get
no consideration and arrive at no un
derstanding with either the shipping
board or the owners, took this con
troversy to the president and said:
'You decide it and we will continue
at work upon whatever conditions you
fix.' But the forces, whatever they
were, that were determined to bring-

AND ENABLE SHIP OWNERS TO EMPLOY CHEAP
FOREIGN LABOR, ANTI-UNIONISTS FORCED

on a crucial contest between the men
and the ship owners of this country,
were too strong to be resisted. The
lockout was put into effect and we
find our sea labor today disorganized,
dissatisfied and rebellious. The con
ditions exist which every enemy of
the American merchant marine must
desire to have exist."

KING DISCUSSES LABOR
Rome.—In his speech at the opening
session of the chamber of deputies
the king of Italy urged that the law
makers strengthen the various co
operative institutions with a view to
promoting other forms of labor asso
ciations that the working classes be
given an opportunity to learn self
government in relation to their eco
nomic life that all social welfare in
stitutions be supported, and the var
ious insurance societies and funds
strengthened that representatives of
all classes be called upon to express
an opinion on the serious labor pro
blem, and that, in carrying out these
recommendations, the strictest impar
tiality be preserved in regard to all
shades of opinion.
CRIED "POOL"
When They Should Have
Clinched Wages
London, England.—It is hinted that
the attack of Frank Hodges, secretary
of the Miners' Federation, against
extremists is the forerunner for the
resignation of that official. Through
out the recent strike, which ended so
disastrously to the miner's, Hodges
was subjected to much criticism from
within the miners' ranks. During the
strike he offered to resign, but it was
not accepted. It is stated that Hodges
will quit rather than be in continual
turmoil over impossible proposals.
He has declared that if the miners'
officials were permitted to use their
judgment they would be in better
shape than now.
"Parrot-like, we were crying 'pool,'
when we should have grasped wages
with both hands," he said.
The proposed "pool" provided that
each ton of coal mined would be as
sessed and the fund placed in charge
of a joint committee that would use
it to equalize wages between high
cost and low cost mines.

waii, there are plenty of unemployed
in the United States without import
ing Chinese coolies, said Victor Olan
der, secretary-treasurer of the Illinois
State Federation of Labor, in a pro
test to congressmen and senators
against the proposal of Hawaiian su
gar planters.

....Washington, D. C.—Coolie impor
tation for peonage on Hawaiian sugar
plantations was given a severe blow
here today when Hawaiian labor rep
resentatives, accompanied by Presi
dent Gompers, of the American Fed
eration of Labor, and fully a dozen
other labor representatives appeared
before the house committee on im
migration.
President George W. Wright, of the
Honolulu Central Labor Union, gave
the committee a complete account of
the plantation labor situation and
made it clear that, entirely aside from
the principle tf Oriental exclusion,
there is no economic excuse for the
importation of coolies.
As important as any statement
made by the labor representatives was
a statement of Congressman Raker, of
California, member of the committee
on immigration.
"The proposed joint resolution, if
adopted," said Congressman Raker,
"would make of Hawaii an island of
criminals. It would let down the bars
to every criminal class everywhere in
the world. And once on American
soil they could go anywhere on Amer
ican soil without passports."
President Wright described planta
tion conditions and told how planta
tion workers had gone into govern
ment works because of better pay.
He made it clear that with the es
tablishment of suitable wage condi
tions the labor supply necessary to
the handling of the sugar crop would
be available. He added that if there
then should be any shortage the Phil
ippine island would furnish the re
quired number of workers. Methods
of plantation operation were severely
criticised from the standpoint of in
efficiency.
The Honolulu Central Labor Union,
it was testified, submitted to the su
gar planters a proposal, or program,
for the relief and remedying of the
labor situation in the islands. This
program contained a diagnosis of the
situation and a remedy therefor as
follows:
"DIAGNOSIS: We find that the
plight in which the sugar industry
finds itself today is due primarily to
the lack of a proper understanding
and co-operation between employer
and employes. This results in the
discouragement and antagonism of the
workers which lowers their efficiency
approximately 50 per cent of the nor
mal, and causes dissatisfaction and
the consequent drifting away from the
plantations of the laborers whose ser
vices are required to take off the crop.
Extremely low wages and the impos
sibility of earning a living except at
the lowest possible standard of sub
sistence prevents those living outside
the plantations from taking plantation
jobs, though there are probably enough
unemployed and temporarily employ
ed who would be willing to fill tho
jobs if the conditions were such that
there was any inducement offered.
"REMEDY: Follow the approved
industrial methods and endeavor to
increase the efficiency of your em
ployes by effecting a right understand
ing: with them. Recognize the prin-

ciple of collective bargaining and sig
nify your willingness to meet with
representatives of your employes in
a conference which we will arrange
and to which we, as representatives of
the Central Labor Council will be pres
ent as the third party acting as me
diators. We will guarantee that if
this is done in the right spirit of co
operation on your part it will be met
in the same spirit by your employes
and an agreement reached whereby
you will gain the friendship of your
men and increase their efficiency to
normal. As one of the chief matters
to be considered at such a conference
we suggest the establishment of a
new wage scale based upon the cost
of living, details as to flexibility and
determining factors to be worked out
later.
"If this increased efficiency does
not then relieve your shortage, and
the return of workers drawn back to
plantation life by incentives offered
still falls short of your requirements,
we believe that plenty of new labor
can be recruited in the Philippines,
with the co-operation of the Filipino
labor commission.
"As an added source of supply we
point out that a large number of men
will be discharged from federal con
struction work upon the signing of
the rehabilitation bill, or shortly
thereafter, who will be forced back on
the plantations where they belong,
provided you make it "a point to em
ploy citizen labor in the skilled and
semi-skilled industries which your
influence dominates. There is no
question in our minds but that you
will thus be able to fill your short
age of labor and maintain your in
dustry at a normal pace without the
importation of any other Oriental la
bor."
Chairman Albert Johnson, conduct
ing the hearing, made what labor rep
resentatives declared to be a studied
effort to entangle President Wright
and to discredit his testimony, but to
no avail. At the conclusion of the
day's testimony, Chairman Johnson
was obliged to compliment the labor
man upon the fairness of his testi
mony.
Under the terms of the coolie im
portation bill, the subject of the hear
ing, it would be possible to import
approximately 50,000 Chinest coolies
to Hawaii upon proclamation that an
emergency exists requiring such la
bor. The coolies would be brought to
the islands in bond and would remain
in a state of peonage in the islands.
It is announced that the American
Federation of Labor and affiliated
bodies will continue a vigorous fight
on the bill, officially known as H. J.
Resolution 171, and that a number of
labor representatives will testify be
fore the committee, objecting at every
step to peonage legislation, letting
down the bars to coolie importation.

FIGHT "BARGAIN" DAYS
Washington.—The theory of "less
government in business" is favor 1
by retailers, but these business men
believe Uncle Sam should aid them
in abolishing "bargain days." Th
A little more than a year ago Henry
Ford bought the Detroit, Toledo and
Iron ton railroad, about 450 miles long
and running through Ohio and Michi
gan. At the time he bought the road
it was, according to the books kept
by the management, losing less bus
iness that it was handling the year
before.
That is one thing that the railroad
milkers who are wrecking other roads
have laid up against Ford. Another
mean thing he did which is considered
an outrage and personal affront by
the aforesaid milkers, was to an
nounce in June that on and after July
1, 1921, the lowest wages to be paid
any employe on the D. T. & I. would
be six dollars for eight hours. The
road is now paying that wage and still
making profits.
The fact that Ford announced the
raise in wages at the same time the
railroad labor board announced that
wages of all railroad workers were to
be cut 12 per cent, which cut was
made at the request of the managers
of other than Ford's road and based
upon their assertions that they could
not pay a wage of $2.92 to unskilled
labor and make a profit, is what hurts.
On July 1, when Ford raised the
lowest wages paid on the D, I. & T. to
$6.00 for a day of eight hours, the
other roads lowered wages to the point
fixed by the railroad labor board from
$2.92 per day to $2.24 per day.
On July 1st and several times since
the railroad managers announced the
fact that there could be no lowering
of freight rates because of the cut in
wages, to the "fact" that the cut was
not deep enough to allow, for a reduc
tion in rates.
Now comes the D. T. & I. and asks
that the freight rates charged by it on
its main line and on all its branches
be cut 20 per cent, beginning August
20th. When the general freight agent
filed the request to be allowed to re
duce 20 per cent with the public util
ities commission at Columbus, Ohio,
the only comment he made in answer
to questions was: "Mr. Ford thought
a reduction should be made."
Can you imagine what the railroad
wreckers who control the rest of the
roads are thinking about Henry?
They have been claiming that they are
operating at a loss and demanding
that the government give them $500,
000,000 or they'd have to go into bank­

Don't get the idea that the Ford

New York.—Striking bookbinders
employed by the T. F. Tapley Com
pany at Long Island City, were sur
prised to learn that their 44-hour
week strike is over. Supreme Court
Justice Gannon made the announce
ment, and issued an injunction against
picketing. The company made the
usual claim to Justice Gannon that its
plant is running at top speed. The
court, thereupon, ended the strike and
affirmed that picketing is unlawful.
The only difficulty with the decree
is that the bookbinders reject the
honorable court's discovery.

BRITISH GUIANA IS
PUCE TO FACE WITH
Crime Among Coolies Is So
Common That Courts Are
Always Crowded
Georgetown, British Guiana, August
24,_(Special.)—B-itish Guiana is about
to come face to face with a serious
labor problem, the result of a decision
made by tho British government dis
continuing the indenture- system, by
which means thousands of East Indian
coolies are now brought to this colony
each year to work on the plantations..
The employers of this kind of labor
are given five years' grace in which
to adjust themselves to the conditions
that will have to be met.
The prosperity and progress of Brit
ish Guiana today are due in no little
measure to the presence of. these cool
ies, who are the backbone of labor in
the island and who enable the planta
tion owners to grow sugar and coca
profitably. The wages of these East
’Indians are lower than any pay re
ceived by other laboring classes in the
western part of the w-orld, and, with
perhaps one or two exceptions, are far
less than any workers in the world
are paid. Without these coolies the
planters of British Guiana will find
the cost of production mounting rapid
ly, for the negroes of the island will
not work for the low wages given to
these Indians. So far in the historv
of this colony it has been necessaiy
for the employers to have cheap labor
in order to get satisfactory returns on
their investments. If the prices on
sugar and cocoa should drop consid
erably with the ending of the inden
ture, the planters would be hard
pressed to grow and market these
products at a reasonable profit.
The coolie as a laborer is a bless
ing to British Guiana, though it is very
questionable whether the system which
brings him here should be permitted to
continue. Opponents of the coolie im
migration plan. who. through their In
fluence, have induced the government
to decide jjgainst it for the future, term
it slavery; and pyrhaps it is, in a mild
meaning of the word. The land pro
prietors and others who have profted

No more striking: instance of the
superiority of the automobile over the
horse and of the ever increasing uses
of the motor vehicle can be found, it
is said, than in the car which 'vyill ap
pear October 7-12, at the Alabama State
Fair.
Just as the high school horse was
an attraction at the fairs and a feature
of the amusement programmes so will
be the machine, known as the car of
many capers, which will give free per
formances every day after the speed
events on the track in front of the
grandstand, and every evening prelim
inary to the big night show.
The trick car, which scientists de
clare to be a freak, and which is the
only one of its kind in the world, has
been booked by Secretary J. E. Dent
as an added attraction, one that has
never been seen here. It will wheel
as nimbly and gracefully, it is said,
through coolie services defend the in
denture with arguments that the East
Indian is better off here than in crowd
ed India, and that his labor is neces
sary to make cultivation profitable.
Both sides have points in their favor.
It is estimated that there now are
more than 100,000 East Indians in
this colony. During the early years
of the indenture system many thou
sands of these laDorers were brought
here with their wives. Rapidly they
multiplied, and the coolie coloney soon'
became a large one. It has continued
to grow fast from year to year, and
is still increasing, in spite of the war,
which has denied the planters full op
portunity to take advantage of their
years of grace. So it is easy to see
how important these Indians are in a
country which depend** upon them for
; labor, and especially when they are a
full third of a total population of 300,.
000 people.
British uiana, like Trfnidad, pays
a heavy toll in the way of a long crim
inal record for having within its boun
daries so much cheap labor. The ne
groes in all the British possessions of
this part of the world are a source of
much trouble to the colonial govern
ment because of their fights, thefts,
disturbances and numeous odd crimes:
but here in this South American col
ony the presence of the East Indian
increases the law violations to a very
high number and gives the police much
to contend with at ail times.
The average East Indian is no men
ace to the peace and safety of the
white man. It is among his own race
that he is a very bad fellow if he hap
pens to be crossed. The coolie inva
riably wants to settle ail differences
himself without awaiting the hand or
the law. He wishes to be his own
judge and executive, and therefore he
is much in court. If a coolie is serious
ly offended by another of his race he
is very apt to satisfy himself by killing
the offending man—or woman. Of if
he fancies he has been wronged in
some trade or in some dealing he either
will wage a fist fight or immediately
go to law with his trouble. Dawyers,
therefore, find the colie a very good
souice of revenue. During the sessions
of court the scenes around the house
of justice in Georgetown are often very
as any waited thoroughbred, and will \

Guided by its inventor and builder, j
Roy Repp of Columbus, O., the acro
batic automobile will glide onto the j
open track, its head and front wheels
aloft. Then by means of an ingenious
gyroscopic arrangement and a tnechan- 1
ism which sets a brake on each of the <
rear wheels, the driver will, while I
the car is upright, put it through its
paces. * i
When the left rear wheel is locked
the car turns easily to the right. When <
the right' wheel is engaged by the ]
brake the turning movement is re- i
versed. The differential gear is a part «
of the apparatus. The gyroscopic con
trivance not only serves to tilt the
car far back but maintains a balance .
which gives the driver perfect control.
Auto polo games will be a daily fea
ture at the fair.

picturesque, for at tjiis time all the ,
cooliej and their families come to
town. The women are dressed in the
gayest of colors and wear all theii
adornments.
Many East Indians are hanged in
British Guiana and Trinidad for mur
der. Among the coolies it is an u?i- i
written law that death must come to I
a wife who commits a cardinal sin. A I
woman is a slave to her master, and
she must obey him in every way. it j
she refuses he more than likely will
disfigure her face; if she betrays him
she is sure to die: provided he learns
of her dishonor; and among these East
Indians news of this sort travels mys
teriously and fast. An East Indian
woman who asserts herself or who
seems to have an air of independence
is very rare—so rare, indeed, that one
may wander through the coolie vil
lages for weeks without meeting such
a woman.

The lawyer was sitting at his desk
absorbed in the preparation of a brief.
So intent was he on his work that he
did not hear the door as it was pushed
gently open, nor see the curly head that
was thrust into his office. A little sob
attracted his notice, and, turning, he
saw a face that was streaked with tears
and told plainly that feelings had been
hurt.
“Well, my little man did you want to
see me?”
“Are you a lawyer?”
“Yes. What do you want?”
“I want"—and there was a resolute
ring in his voice—“I want a divorce rrom
my papa and mamma.”

Perhaps some day a master of the
en who can dress up facts to look
ke fiction will tell the story of a
ian’s day in terms of transportation,
f that ever does happen the average
lan may gain at least a faint realiza
on of what transportation means to
im and to the human race.
Beginning with the newspaper and
he milk left at the ordinary citizen's
oor in the morning and ending with
he billow on which he lays his head
t night, the ‘story will bring home to
he reader an understanding of his de
endence upon waterways, railways
nd highways for everything he uses,
‘hen he will think of his morning cof
ee—for a moment, at least—not as
brew of berries from the corner gro
ery. but as a drink for which the
our.clatlon was brought overland by
rail and-rail, down rivers and across
ceans, over country roads and through
ity streets. He will visualize the
housands of miles of transport in
olved in bringing to his table even
he simplest of breakfasts; he will
ollow in his mind the divers go
ngs and comings of men to produce
he clothes he wears; he will picture
he converging trails from mine, for
>st and field, over which there have
leen brought the component parts ot
he innumerable things he uses in his
lay’s work.
Only such a word picture will ever
Irive home to the rank and file of
lumanity the fact that transportation
lystems make up the foundation of
>very-day life; that without them we
vould be no further advanced than
savages in*the wilderness. Nothing
;lae can ever so forcefully illustrate
nan’s dependence upon the agencies
hat fetch and carry for him,, can make
lim go fully appreciate his debt to
hose of his fellows who establish and
:are for the routes of trade and travel.
When thlB story has been written—
ufssibly in a 'Connected narrative like
.hat outlined, but more likely In a
nultitude of word sketches—the people
md those set in authority over them
nay comprehend the tremendous possi
bilities in the nation’s highways. The
•oads are thefe. some ready for use
ind some yet ^ he put into usable
jondition, but all having a potential
calue literally incalculable and ini
neasurable.
Good roads are said to have saved
France. What can they not do for
\merica?—Editorial from Good Road’s',
New York, issue of August 10, 1918.

ibout 15 minutes ago.”

ranklin K. Lane, Secretary of the In

ot have ventured to suggest to Mr.

leans committee, that In preparing the
ew tax bill It would be the wiser part

nd excess profits of corporations

:ain life are not allowed to amortize
the actual Investment before being
subject to the excess profits tax. The
nen who are otherwise willing to de
'elop such properties fear they will not
ie able to get their investment back."
ADMINISTRATION’S WARNING
This statement is spoken of here as
perhaps the most significant and im
portant of any that have come from the
administration referring to the pro
posed new tax law, excepting of course,
he able and carefully thought-out
statement which was made a few day?
igo by Secvretary McAdoo and which
seems to have convinced the ways and
neans committee or its chairman that
the Secretary’s view is correct.
Excess profits taxes may, it is
feared, be carried so far as not only to
impede the enlargement of industries
now occupied in providing necessary
minerals for war purposes, but also
other important industrial proposi
tions.
THE POTASH QUESTION
The report made in this column a
few days ago which referred to the
development of the potash industry in
the United States has brought forth
a number of communications from tlu.
managers of leading industries in this
country, all of which refer to the im
portance of calling the attention of
this country to the political as well as
industrial necessity of building up the
potash industry in the United States.
Dr. Charles Catlett, who is one of the
foremost of American chemists anil
who since the war began has given a
great deal of thought and much Inves
tigation to the question of the possi
bility of securing potash in cement and
in Iron making, in a communication
states that among the greatest poten
tial sources for potash production are
the blast furnaces, and yet these fur
naces are now pushed to the limit by
the demand for iron, so that the man
agers are unable to listen to any sug
gestion that the bi-products of the fui
naces be utilized for the recovery of
potash.
John J. Porter, who is first vice pres
ident and also general manager of one
of the largest cement industries of the
United States, which has been for some
years in successful operation at Ha
gerstown, Md., states in his communi
cation that there is no question but
that it is the intention of Germany to
use her supposed potash monopoly as
one of the means of enforcing the best
peace terms possible.
Mr. Porter is of the same opinion
which is held in Washington and in
business circles of this city that not
only ‘will it be to the industrial advan
tage of the United States that we se
curo Independence so far as potash is
cocnerned, but also will be a matter of
great political importance.
The cement manufacturers have done
wonders in the way of utilizing bi
products of the industry for securing
potash. Mr. Porter, while agreeing
with Dr. Catlett, that one of the chief
potential sources of potash in *the
United-States is in the blast furnace, is
persuaded that the cement Industry of
fers an even greater field. The Cuban
ores, which are used by the Bethlehem
Steel company, and the gray hematites
of eastern Alabama are ores which

Ii is jnst 3 fallacious to stjls ll th
Chinamen wbo are amongst as " Cool.

more so, in fact ; for, really there are
oo " Coolies " in the United States
The fact is that the so called " Coolie
trade " has never been opened between
any American port and China; although
there doubtless have been some bun
dreda of Chinamen brought to the
United States who, prior to their leav
ing China, bad entered into contracts.
at certain fixed rates of payment, to per.
form for a given period of time, a cer
tain sort of labor. But of this there
are few, if anj, certain proofs, much as
we bear of the cobtroliiog power of
"The See Top" and other Chines:
companies who have business bead
Quarters in San Francisco. But there
seems to be danger fiat tie facility with
which Chinamen, in large bodies can be
made parties to contracts to perform cer
tain kinds of labor in this country will
be promotive of soch abuses as ought to
be provided against. One Koopmaoschap
has lately figured largely as a contractor
for furnishing gangs of Chinese laborers
fjr plantation and other labor at the
Sontb. While this is not strictly
'Coolie" labor, there are good grounds
for apprehension that, after the China
sien whom ilr. Kaop man3cbap proposes
to furnish to the planters shall have
once been set at work in tbe cotton and
cane Gelds of Dixie, the old slave over
seers who will be put over tbem will
treat tbem as tbey csed to treat tbe
b'.acks, and very soon come to regard
tbem as a class without rights which
either white men or black men are bound
U respect. Great bod.es ot laborers
wbo may be under contract to perform
specific undertaking, like tbe building
of a railway, do not incur the danger
which exists in plantation life for there
t'ae labor is perpetual tbe "job" is
never finished. Most wisely, tbe treaty
negociated by Seward and Burlingame
between the United Slates and China
rohibits the importation cf laborers
under contracts ; and if anything ap
proaching Cojlieism should possibly
creep into tbe management of Chinese
laborers in the United Stttes it will ex
ist in direct violation of law. We are
triad to note that tbe Senate has under
::s consideration a bill which looks to
ta preservation of any attempt to
trodnce servile laVir into the country.
A synopsis of tba. bill, furnished by
telegraph, says of it:
It provides that every contract for labor,
any part of the consideration of which shall
be moaey fuc transportation advanced for any
alien from any foreign country, which pro
vides lor a longer period ot service man six
months, shall be declared to be a contract fur
servile labor, and contrary to public policy
and punishes any infraction by heavy fines.
Tbe intent of this is a good one , and
sicS a act would protect lie unite
no I E jroneao alike wit'a the Chinese
laborer. The point songht to be attained
is to prevent tbe reduction of any class of
laborers to a roiditioo of real or virtu!
slavery. Our ports are as open to Chi
beat as to European immigration. Tbry
who would come, may ; and once here
they are (jure as filly entitled to the
rrotectioo of tbe lairs and tt an eo.u:
administration of ibrro (as gnarsnteed
in tbe Fif.eenth Amendment Bit') as all
other clashes of our inhabitants. In
claiming boaest justice to tbe Chinamen
one must protest against Coolie labor.
Coolie labor, or anything that imitates
it, is, to all inten's and purposes, slave
! bor. To presuppose all Chinamen to
ie Coolies and to deny then a right to
rompete with other Uborers is quite a
unjust as to permit tbem to be heldander
m contract svS'em wbicb redact them to
critical alavery. Tbese peculiar peo
pie are amo-igsi as in large numbers ;
and while we are working oat tbe diffl
(.all problem of what to do with tbem,
me try worst possible obstacle which
ra be placed ia tbe way of a just and
Itiaiie solution of it is to give conn.
Laaace to such a treatment of them as
baa beea too frequently manifested by
savage tempered, intolerant organisa
tions and indinduals ja various parts
f tbe Pacifie States. To treat a Cbma
maa like a slave is aol tbe way to pre.
vent bis being beld as a Coolie. i
When Life Insurance was a tree of meager
growth in this country, it would have been a
very difficult task to persuade its most san.
guine supporters, that a quarter of a century
would produce such results from such small
beginnings. When we reflect that more has
been done in the United States, than in the
crowded capitals of Europe, where Assurance
of laves, in one form or another, bas been
practiced for more than a century, we can ap
preciate something of that prudent foresight.
and careful calculation of chances which is
characteristic of the Yankee mind, though
he is oftea set down as being reckless and in
the extreme.
Some men succeed in business while others
fail, but we are glad to know that in Life In
surance there is no such word as fail, for.
while the laws of mortality and interest remain
as ther have been for the past two centuries,
so long may we rest assured that when we
have insured our lives for the benefit of others,
we have done one of the things we ought to
have done, and that we have acted wisely and
well. A Company which has grown with
such steady progress, and with such swift in
crease that it seems impossible that such re
sults have been so speedily realized, is the
Continental Life Insurance Company of New
York. In May. 1866, its first policy was issu
ed; in December, 18G9, over twenty thousand
had been written. The increase of business,
as indicated by the number of policies issued
in IS39 over the year 1SGS, is greater than is
reported by any other Company in the world.
A very remarkable feature in the history of
this Company is the extremely low ratio of
mortality, as shown by the Reports to the
Massachusetts Commissioner for the years
of 1S67 and 1S53 inclusive ; in fact, lower
than any other Company represented in the
report. While the average rate of losses in
all the Companies was ttcenty per cent., that
of the Continental was only nine per cent.
This fact is worthy of attention, as the rate of
mortality is one of tbe most important and
peculiar considerations which affect the stand.
ing and dividends of a Life Company. By
consent of the stockholders, the Company is
now conducted on the mutual plan as far as
the policy-holders are concerned, all the sur
plus being divided on the contribution plau.
By this pi in, now adapted by the best Com
panies, such a share of the policy-holder's
money is returned as belongs to him, and the
longer he is a member the larger will his sur
plus become, or what is the same thing, his
payments will materially diminish. Grace to
the extent of thirty davs is extended to the
policy-holder, end the policy is held good
during the time. A favor of this kind costs
the Company nothing, but under some cir
cumstances it may save the policy when it
may be most valuable.
After paving expenses, losses, and divid
ends, the balance of assets on the 31st of De
cember, 1S69, belonging solely to the policy
holders, was over Three-and-One-Half-Mil-lions
of Dollars. Such a success has only
been attained by energy, activity, and patience
on the part of the officers and agents of the
Continent!. Some of tbem were men of ex
perience when they joined hand in hand to
posh this Company. Others were men who
seem to have been wonderfully well qualified
fox the positions which they have filled with
so much credit.
If a success so significant has been achiev
ed so speedily, and the same talent and tact
conspire, the day is net far distant when this
vigorous sapling may overshadow all the
other oaks in the forest.

Yesterday's Enterprise contains the fol
lowing report of a daring feat by high
waymen :
Two masked men walked into tbe of
fice of the Devil's Gate Toll Road at
half-past 8 o'clock last evening, and,
presenting cocked revolvers at Captain
Taylor, tbe agent and half owner of the
road, commanded mm to produce the
keys of the safe and open it. The safe
happened to be open when one of the
robbers pulled out the drawers, and
taking out tbe money found in them put it
into a sack which he found with consid
erable coin in it, tbe other robber mean
while keepiug his pistol presented at
Mr. Taylor. Tbey obtained $807 in all.
Having obtained tbe money, one of the
robbers started off up the road with it!
while the other for a time stood guard
over 31 r. Taylor, telling him be would
blow bis brains out if he moved or gave
any alarm, r inally this fellow also left,
and the alarm was at once given, and
soon quite a crowd was collected about
the Toll-bonse, which stands in the
Devil's Gate, just at tbe upper end of
Silver City, and with mills and build
ings close about it. Tbe Captain bad
in bis pockets, wbich the robbers
did not get, as tbey did not search bim.
Tbis is the boldest robbery we have
heard of in a longtime. No trace of tbe
robbers had been obtained np to a late
boar last nigbt.

N BOARD sailing ships
the social life of the
seamen is concen
trated into the sec
ond dog: watch. That
name alons ; gests
to the sailor's ear a
convivial time when
the day's work is done, and he may
sit lazily around, smoking, singing
and spinning yarns.
Nobody ever mentions the first dog
watch; it is the second, the two hours
from 6 till 8 in the evening, just after
supper, that, means as much to Jack
as the 5 o'clock tea or the evening
reception does to the society swells
ashore. Then the most crabbed of
sailors will thaw out and display con
versational ability that would be en
vied by many a man in higher walks
of life.
Sea life develops a keen sense of
humor, and this enters strongly into
the social talks aboard ship. Each
mantis willing to be made the butt
of his shipmates' jokes and to have
all his little failings presented in a
humorous light, if he knows that it
will afford general amusement. But
his. replies in defense are not apt to
lack the sting of sarcasm.
On American ships the second dog
watch is not made so much of
American ships officers drive their
men in truly American fashion. It
was on a British ship, a limejuicer,
that I first learned the full meaning
of the second dog watch. In the
British Merchant Marine that time is
held more sacred from interference
than Sunday services.
There were twenty of us in the fore
castleten men on each watch. In
fine weather all hands had the even
ing meal together at four bells 6 in
the evening.' The glow of social con
tentment came after the mess tins
were cleared away.
' There were men of almost all na
tions among us, but all had the com
mon yarning ability of the sailor.
Billy the Swede could tell of the
Greenland whaling fleets, Cockney
Joe could spin out tales of soldier life
in India, and Italian Pete educated us
on the fisherman's life in the Bay of
Biscay. Then there was a chap ffom
Portland, Maine, who was always go
ing to tell us how he managed a farm
in the Pine Tree State, but always
What Blocks Sidewalks Worst of Jill
TT TT AVE you ever noticed," ask
ed the policeman at the
iL iL busy city crossing, when
there was a momentary
lull in the traffic, "what causes the
biggest blocks on the sidewalks in our
big cities? It is the sight of a man
doing some 'stunt on top of a sky
scraper. "Nothing else causes a crowd to
gather so quickly and stay so long.
When a man gets out on top of the
Park Row Building, the tallest sky
scraper in America, and paints the
flagstaff four hundred feet above the
ground, a squad of police cannot keep
the sidewalk clear. Even when a man
gets outside one of the top stories to
clean the windows, there is sure to be
a crowd watching him.
"It is the same when a steeplejack
climbs up to the top of a church
steeple. Men who have rushed out
of their offices as if their worst enemy
were after them with a gun, and have
wolfed a couple of bananas and a glass
of milk at the hfnch counter, will stop
on their way back to the otYice and
watch that steeplejack for a quarter of
an hour, as if time were no object to
them.
"Women are even worse. They are
absolutely fascinated. They feel dead
Eating One's Gods.
The god- of a South American tribe
of Indians are generally looked upon
as sacred. But when the gods become
a nuianee, it is time to put an end
to them. At least, that is what a cer
tain tribe along the Yabari River think.
They worship the iguana and monkey,
but as soon a the iguana started
eating chickens they thought it time
to stop worshipping him, and tn eat
him in. place of the chickens he had
eaten, f his they do, and they prefer
iguana stew to chicken fricassee.

make, too. 'Poor man,' one sympa

'Would he be killed before he struck

"Considering that the steeplejack

I was sorry the equipment of a city

Sympathetic Colors.
Science has made the discovery that
every person is sympathetic to some
particular color. This being ths case
it is important that every person
should learn what that color is. Ner
vous persons are said to find blues
and greens soothing, while reds and
yellow best suit the emotional tem
perament. The brain worker will find
it beneficial to use a shade of his or
her special color over the rcadine
lamp.

SOME FOREIGN
CELEBRITIES
L
ONG sermons are the German
Emperor's special aversion, and
court chaplains are obliged to
condense their discourses so
that the delivery will only consume
fifteen minutes.
The King of Italy detest! cards and
will not touch them, owing to the fact
that his father wasted hours over
them.
Since the death of Alphonso XII.
card playing has been forbidden at
the court of Spain.
The King of Spain's pet pastime is
kite fighting. A game called the
"Knights of Spain" is also another
one of his sports. It is something
like polo, only on a less dashing scale.
The Grand Duke of Hesse is never
so happy as when he can' snatch a
momtnt froin affairs of state to de
vote to his embroidery. He is very
skilful with his needle, and his work
is described as beautiful. He takes
the greatest interest in it, and is par
ticularly clever in the arrangement of
colors.
The King of England is an, expert
shoemaker, a trade he chose to learn
when a boy. He was obliged by his
royal parents to learn some business,
he his even worn shoes of his own
making. The late King Humbert of
Italy was also a royal cobbler.
Demitasse In a Small Cup.
The jargon of restaurants wa new
to' him, but he had ambitions, an obr
servant eye and an acute ear. So he
soon came to feel he could order his
dinner with as much grace as the next
man, and thought himself of the elect
when he called out to the waiter:
"Some demitasse coffee, waiter, and
bring it in a small cup."

THE LOUVRE
IN DANGER
-a

HE offices of the Department
of the Colonies of France are
situated in a portion of the
Louvres Museum called the
"Pavillion de Flore" at the end of the
great building on the side facing the
Seine and the Garden of the Tuileries.
This proximity of the government of
fices to the art treasures of France
and to a certain extent of the whole
world has often been the case of se
rious complaints on the part of the
French artistic world.
In the winter the Colonial Office
is heated with stoves and grate fires,
and civil servants smoke cigarettes
more or less openly in Parisian gov
ernment buildings. , It goes without
saying that in case of fire twenty min
utes would be sufficient to destroy
art. treasures which could never be
replaced.
A remedy would seem hopeless
were it not for the fact that the Bud
get Committee have put their feet
down firmly, and given the Minister
of the Colonies to understand that
he and his pumerous staff must move
to the Champ de Mars, where accom
modation is provided in the shape of
buildings used during the exposition
of iooo.
Provided that fire does not break
out in the meantime in the present
Ministry for the Colonies, the Louvre
may be considered safe and saved.

"Naw," corrected some one, "you
had got to where you was so jagged
that ye thought yerself in Calcutta
when ye was in Portland, Maine."
"And you vas lookin' in de mirror
over de bar," broke in Dutch Charley,
"und ven von see yourself, you tink
it was a Hindoo cooley. You got to
verc you vas grinnin' at yourself."
"Never mind. Buck," said Italian
Pete, soothingly, "you wasa tell how
you wasa doin most unusual ting
you wasa taka drink. Go ahead.
Buck."
Buck looked about him pityingly, as
though in sorrow at the grade of hu
man beings he was obliged to associ
ate with.
"When I come to," he continued,
ignoring all interruptions, "I found
myself in a most wonderful kind o
garden, where the trees was different
from any I'd ever seen before, an
there was flowers of all colors, an'
in the middle was a fish pond, the
water o' which was red like blood. I
riz up, an' so help me Bob, I had on
wot seemed to be silk pajamas, but
wasn't; an' on my feet I had red slip
pers, an' puttin' my hand to my head,
I took off a most wonderful lookin
cap full o' gold and lace. Now, you
must know, I was voung in those
days, an' not a half bad lookin' chap.
Well, I reaches into my inside pocket
where usually I kept a small hand
mirror, an' pulls it out, and would ye
believe me, I was dressed and looked
like one o' them swell maha-rajahs."
"There must a' been a powerful
change in yer looks," broke in one of
the men.
Buck ignored the remark and went
on.
"Well, I got up, an' walked around,
an' pretty soon ip comcs a fancy

big frypan without a handle. After
salaamin' to me a good deal, he sez:
"'Will ye have a drink, sahib?'
"'Corse I will, sez I.
"An with that I drinks off the finest
cocktail as ever I tasted.
" 'Well,' sez I after drinkin,' 'what's
this game? How t'ell did I get here?'
" 'Why,' sez the coolie, 'me mistress,
the rajah's daughter, is wantin' to see
yez.' "
"Say, Buck," broke in one of his

grinned at ye over the bar?"

"whereabouts does the English ad
miral come in as was asking, yer the

contains a collection of pens which,
while interesting from a connoisseur's
point of view as works of art, are no
less attractive to the historian.
Every time a sovereign visits the
Town Hall in Paris he is begged to
sign his name in the Visitors' Book,
and for this purpose a 'richfy-orna-mented
pen is handed the royal vis
itor. For the intended visit of the King

"You fellers," retorted Buck, "is a
most thankless lot o' bums to tell
a story to, 'cause yer ignorance is a
cloudin' yer intellects. Now, if I was
to tell ye "a story of the Bowery or
Whitechapel, I would be comin' down
to your level, but a chap like me, wot
has knocked around the world an'
seen the swell people of all lands, is
not appreciated by a crowd o yokels
like you. However, I shall do the best
I can so's yer lop-sided brains can
grasp the substance o my yarn.
"Let's see, I was tellin ye of"
"That ye woke up in a patch of wa
termelons with red flowers a growin'
over 'em," prompted one of the men.
"Was I?" reflected Buck. "Well,
seein' as how melons don't grow in
Rangoon, that was a most extraordi
nary incident."
"How did ye get to Rangoon,
Buck?" queried Billy the Swede. "Vas
the English admiral take you dere in
his flagship?"
"Come, fellers," admonished an old
salt, "don't be a joshin' Buck when
he's a tryin' to improve our minds
with his truthful yarns of life in the
Orient. Buck, these chumps is a
guyin' ye, but I have the greatest con
fidence in yer truthfulness. Ye was
tellin', Buck, how ye woke up in a
public park in Calcutta, arter a booze
as would make any able seaman proud
of hisself, an' a Sikh policeman was a
tryin to lead ye oft to the station
house. Ye was givin a most vivid de
scription of how the trees looked red
an' the fountain was a spoutin port
wine, which, arter all, was most
natural, with a head like you must a
had. 1 been there myself, an' I knows
how it feels. Go ahead, Buck."
But the thread of Buck's narrative
had been broken.
"You chaps," he said, "is not capa
ble of understandin' my high class
story tellin. Hereafter, I shall have
to keep my yarns down to booze
shops, above which yer imaginations
carnt rise."
One bell struck. Buck arose to go
into the forecastle to prepare himself
for relieving the wheel.
"Say, Buck," shouted Pete as a part
ing shot, "you raisa blue turnip an
pink watermelon on your farm, like
wasa grow in dat garden you wake
up in?
Ten minutes later eight bells
brought an end tq further talkrand all
hands mustered aft, after which the
watch not on duty turned in.

During the midday heat the young
hunter employs other methods. He
leaves the hounds at home and seeks
the sequestered nooks where the bucks
are used to lying stretched out at full
length.
, At night, when the moon is out, he
lies hidden beside the drinking pools,
always to leeward, and when the der
come to drink he bags his game. .
There is an unwritten law among
California backwoodsmen which
makes the killing of a doe or spotted
fawn a serious offense. The day a
young buck loses his spots he be
comes legitimate game.
The State game laws are almost
completely ignored. Even the sheriff
of a backwoods county is not always
above hitting the trail of a buck some
moonlight night In districts where
houses are twenty and thirty mile
apart, game laws must necessarily be
a dead letter.
' Sparrows in Tunis.
In France and, in fact, in most
European countries, sparrows are
commonly considered useful to the
agriculturist on account of the quan
tities of caterpillars and other insects
which they destroy.
In Tunis, however, this is not the
case. There these little birds have
been found to be so destructive to the
crops that they are declared by the
Government to he "outlaws." The
French Government in Tunis has gone
even further than this, and has issued
proclamations to the effect that not
only sparrows be destroyed by every
one, but that they must be extermi
nated under penalty of a fine in the.
case of those who neglect this public
duty;

From Maxwell's Talisman.
If our agriculture is to continue in
the1 future as it has been in the past
founded on sound democratic princi
ples, the large farm of today must
give place to small farms more in
tensively tilled. Changed conditions
necessitate . a reorganization of our
agriculture to meet the three-fold de
mand of increased crop production,
greater capacity for population and
more free homes to supply the ni-
j tion with a vigorous and democratic
citizenship. . Compared with the
densely populated rural districts of
Europe and all the older nations of
the world,, .the country here in Ameri
ca Is an uninhabited wilderness. And
this while our -cities are filled to ov
erflowing and the population in them
is admittedly too congested for the
public good. Not the west alone, but
eastern states , like . New York . and
Massachusetts are largely uninhab
ited from a rural standpoint. A few
dots on the map Indicate crowded
mltffona; nd" f 1 ' the great gxpsfcnse
between these sustains but a sprink
ling of people. Blot , out the' factory
towns and trade centers of; thesd two
states, and - what r would this popula
tion be? .Nor does, this indicate that
because fewer people live onr-the land
the net" crop production is all .the
greater and that In a measure the
concentration of population in cities
necessitates a meager rural popula
tion in order that all may be fed.
Quite the contrary. The maximum
yield would be immeasureably in
creased if we transformed our agri
cultural districts to an intensive far
ming basis, and improbable, as it may
at first thought seem, this would en
able the rural districts, while sup
porting an immensely greater rural
population, to export more product
than they at present do to sustain
the people of the cities.
Present conditions of unequal dis
tribution of population between city
and country are bound to have
very vital bearig on the people as
a whole. The evil results are far-
reaching and indirectly affect many
phases of our national life. Inade
quate rural population and hte conse
quent failure to replenish the cities
and recruit the ranks of industrial
labor is the real reason for the in
flow of hordes of able-bodied men
from the agricultural districts of Eu
rope, and for the lesser, but no less
significant immigration from, over
flowing rural Japan. The Chinese in
flux has been only artiflcally check
ed and already there are signs of a
change of policy towards the Intro
duction of coolie labor. Why boast of
our ungainly farms and sparsely pop
ulated rural districts and ranches that
export beef t oEurope but furnish no
homes for citizens, if these are .the
real causes of the immigration evil?
If we 'do not soon heed the sign and
put our own people on the land that
is now ours, we will not at a latter
day be able to stay the hand of fate
that passes our rich heritage on to
other races.
. We live in a rich and partially de
veloped -country where capital Is
plentiful and labor Is scarce. .Bound
less agricultural resources remain
yet to be developed. But today capital
is waiting upon labor. Why is it that
we fail to produce this labor and de
velop our resources without the ne
cessity; of importing alien labor? We
fail because the overwhelming major
ity of our people live in cities under
conditions that preclude a rapid In
crease in population. It is the dense
ly populated rural districts of the
world that have produced the men
for war and for industry through all
the ages. While England yet had a
vigorous rural stock we , enjoyed a
generous inflow, of English immigra
tion. English agriculture has atrop
hied and today the alien hordes en
tering our great seaports come no
longer front England, but from the
densely populated rural districts of
Italy, Austro-Hungary and Japan. The
railroad section gang is the pulse of
our immigration 'and today we see
they are in the east Italians, Palish
and Hungarians, in the southwest,
Mexican, and in the west and "north
west, Japanese. Aother instance is
in the fruit orchards and celery fields
of California where Chinesie coolie
labor finds employment. And today
comes the cry from the Portland,
Oregon, Chamber of Commrece that
we admit annually Chinese coolie la
bor to the extent of one-tenth of one
per cent of our population at the
time. That would mean 80,000 China
men the first year and an increasing
number each succeeding year. It is
argued that we need unskilled labor
and that the development of the coun
try awaits the introduction of this
class. They say these laborers can
not compete with skilled white labor
This contention would not be borne
out in practice, for the Chinese are
not an inferior people in comparison
with us, and though the coolie is a
a coolie so long as he stays in over
crowded China, where there are no
opportunities, he no sooner reaches
this country and accustoms himself
to the new ways than he displays
the advantages of his remarkable
training and thrift and Industry. No
one who has seen the Chinese cool
ie turned cook, excel his white breth
ren and crowd out all competitors,
or apply his native skill In agricul
ture and monopolize the field In mar
ket gardening, can believe that the
Chinese coolie will never compete
with skilled labor. It is more a ques
tion whether he would not' eventual
ly completely displace our own skill
ed labor.
But granting that coolie labor and
other alien races will not deprive our
own people of an occupation, are
we assured "that their importation
will be unattented hy other evils?
Two centuries ago the southern
planters imported unskilled labor to
enable them to expend their cotton
raising industry. From the standpoint
of the wealthy planters this brought
nothing but benefit. But today we
have the poor whites and the negro
question as the result of ' this short
sighted policy. Who can say that the
descendants of those planters have
not paid and do not suffer from Ihis
unfortunate solution of the labor pro
blem in the south two hundred years
ago? What assurance have we that
there is no unforeseen menace in the
ever-increasing inflow of alien races
on both coasts and from over the
Mexican border, beyond our powers
of assimilation? This is a democracy
and it will be in peril when we have
an alien problem brought ' on by the
presence of a numerous body of infer
ior and unassimilated foreigners in
our midst.
We are short of labor because our
own tremendous activity in a land
hardly touched as yet has created an
immense demand for them, and while
we have created a great market for
labor we have entirely overlooked
the necessity of supplying the demand
So we have to look to foreign coun
tries at this day and accept an In
ferior class of labor that must even
tually have a radical effect on Ameri
can society and government. A con
tinued inpouring of this alien labor
is a serious menace to "us and a dan
ger to our government. We have
brought on" a national crisis and our
future hangs on our solution of the
problem. It s for us to decide wheth
er we shall import labor or produce
our own. With a population greater
than that of Germany and France
combined, occupying? half a contin
ent, and admittedly the wealthiest
people in the world, why is it that
we fail to produce population, show
ing a birth rate lower than ' that of
most civilized countries of the world,
France along' excepted? Let us not
be blinded by the fingures of swell
ing population that are largely af
fected by immigration, which account
ed for an increase of 1,000,000 last
yearl . alone. We are even in some
danger of being ousted by what was
formerly a ' hand full . of Mormons
who with greater foresight , founded
homes on the land while we dissipat
ed our energies in the cities. The rea
son for our shortage of labor lies in
our failure to recognize the principle
that the nation must be recruited '
from the soil ; and the nations that
are today rooted to the soil are now i
silently pressing In amongst us nn- i
til the system that has left agricultur
in the hands of a scanty farming
population and glorified 1 the wealthy
farmer rather than a wealth of far
ers. We cannot stop immigration ar
tiflcally, but we can forestall it by
supplying the demand for labor from
homes on the land. Our only recourse
is in a reorganization of agriculture
population of intensive farmers liv
ing on their own land. This would
be far better than a surrender o the
alien labor and unwittiingly sowing
the germ that would later drive out
our own race.
The constitution . requires us to
produce our own president, and wo
manage somehow to live up to this.
Would it not be wise to raise our
own rank and file? Every large em
ployer of labor as well as every wage
earner should take this to heart and
preach and support "homes on the
land" as a patriotic duty as well for
the direct personal advantage. JuPt
in so far as the citizens of today are
tenement dwellers and the- scum and
froth of our cities, will our popula
tion of tomorrow be recruited from
rural districts. If these lie beyond
our borders, then that part of oiir
population will be alien. These for
eign immigrants are not an unmix
ed evil, but we can produce better
material in our own country if we
only will. Japan has just closed a re
markable war "fought by prearranjre
ment," and given the world a lesson
In foresight and forethought. Are
we to profit by this and apply just
such intelligent methods . to our own
problems, or are we to let things take
care of themselves only to see our
selves later submerged under a tidal
wave o fforeign immigration coming
to fill the positions we created but
failed to fill? It is high time we learn
ed from the Japanese to do things
"by prearrangement." With the labor
problem it Is now or never, so far '
as we are concerned.
The manufacturer who advocates
foreign immigration because he needs
men and cannot get them, is not to
blame. We are all to blame, because
we have a republic and the respon
sibility for its faulty workings -ests
on aH of us alike; and likewise the
duty of improving our government
and methods rests on every citizen.
What we want today is not quibbling
and hair-spitting about immigration,
but robust action that will eliminate,
the immigration question for all
time.
American agriculture filled the bill
in its day, but modern industrialism
has made it inadequqate, not that
the country fails to feed the nation,
but because it fails to give us citi
zens to supply the increasing demand.
Labor and capital must unite , their
energies to carry through the grand
policy of homes on the land, plant
ing the small farm Intensively tilled
and rooting labor to the soil where it
will gather new strength and be a
greater resource to the nation than
the factory labor of today is.
Don't Borrow Trouble. '
It is a bad habit to borrow any
thing, but" the worst thing you can
possibly : borrow, is trouble. . When
sick ,sore, heavy weary and worn
out by the pains and poisons of dys
pepsia, biliousness, Bright's Disease,
and similar internal disorders, don't
sit down and brood over your symp
toms, but fly for relief to Electric Bit
ters. Here you will find sure and

CHINESE STYLE
IN ELECTRICS TO
BE USEDJNU. S.
Seat in Rear for Footman May
be Offered by Milburn Com
pany. . ; i
China with which we usually asso
ciate hand laundries, chop sticks and
the first printing press is responsible
for an innovation In the building of
modern electric automobiles, says
Harry Chenoweth of the Chenowetli
Auto Co., dealer in Milburn electrics.
To satisfy demands made by the
Chinese elite, the Milburn Wagon com
pany of Toledo, builders of the Mil
barn electric, are equipping all cars
destined for China with what is known
as the "coolie" seat.
These are mounted on the rear and
are Intended for coolie attendants,
without whom none of the upper class
appears in public.
The coolie resembles, so far as his
duties are concerned, the more or less
obsolete footman who rode on the box
or on a high seat In the rear of the
coaches fashionable In the days of our
forebears.
Coolie Sits Behind
While the car is in motion the coolie
sits perched upon the seat built espe
cially for him. As soon as it come3
to a stop he must dismount, open the
door of the car, help the occupants
to alight and then stand guard until
their return. After the car's occupants
have again taken their places and their
every comfort is , assured, the coolie
remounts to his position, often being
forced to do so while the car is in
motion.
The new seats are made as comfort
able for the attendant as possible. The
seat is cushioned and it is supported
in front by springs. Handles have
been added at the sides to eliminate
the possibilities of losing the attend
ant at some sharp turn, and a foot-rest
also has been provided. Solicitude for
the comfort of the coolie practically
ends there, however. When it rains
he is liable to a drenching.
Scores of these are being built for
the Chinese trade by the Milburn com
pany which for the last several years
has done an extensive business in tlio
Asiatio country.
It is not anticipated by Milburn of
ficials that the American public will
follow the style established by the
Chinese.
REPUBLIC BEGINS
PEACE PRODUCTION
"The problem of turning our fac
tories to the manufacture of peace
products is not nearly so serious as
that which confronted us in convert
ing from a peace to a war basis," says
F. W. Ruggles, president of the Re
public Motor Truck company, Inc.,
Alma, Michigan.
"Our continuous production of Re
public trucks throughout the war, on
a greatly restricted scale, of course,
and the fact that our work for the
government was practically confined
to the production of standard trucks,
enables us to make the post-war tran
sition with little or no change in our
manufacturing facilities.
"The elimination of government
work from Plant 1, an important unit
of the Republic factories, has permit
ted that plant to resume work exclu
sively on trucks for commercial use.
The last big consignment of standard
three-and-a-half ton Republic war
trucks left our factories just before
beginning of the new year. There are
still a number of Republic trucks
being built under government speci
fications at our military plant. These
should be completed and ready for
delivery the middle of next month.
This will clear our decks entirely and
enable us to put through our exten
Hive production plans without inter
ruption. "When the history of the motor
truck industry is written, the year just
passed, will probably be pointed out
as the year in which the motor truck
came Into its own.
" No one industry was more in the
public eye during 1918, or the year
previous than the motor truck indus
try. Given Its big opportunity by the
.world war and the consequent neces
sity for bettering transportation fa
cilities, the motor truck immediately
made good. And now even the most
skeptical will readily concede that the
field of motor truck usefulness has
barely been scratched and that the
new giant industry is bound to grow
to even greater proportions.
"And now, taking all things into
consideration, I believe that the year
ahead of us will be by far the biggest
year we have ever known."

OXFORD Prof. Samuel J. Branden
burg, librarian of Miami university,
has been granted leave of absence for
six months, and will engage in work
for the division of vocational rehab
ilitation, under the direction of the
federal board of vocational education.
He will visit army camps in Ohio, In
diana and Kentucky, and will assist
in placing wounded soldiers in places
of employment where they are best
fitted.
Charles Smoots, night marshal of
this village, was arrested yesterday
on a warrant sworn out by Jonas
Doaks, colored, charging interference
between husband and wife. The war
rant was issued in the court of Squire
Peter Messel, of Hanover township,
south of here. Smoots is also a col
ored man. The affair has .caused con
siderable comment as the defendant is
an officer. The trial will come off
today. It is understood that the case
is largely one of domestic troubles,
and is more or less complicated.
EATON Preble county's draft
board has ceased operations ' and is
awaiting formal disbandment by the
head of the selective service system.
The board's wark ended Friday, when
its records, securely packed, were
started on their way to Washington.
A order issued some days ago direct
ed that all local boards throughout
the country must cease functioning
after next Monday, March 31, but that
members of boards would be retained
for a short time thereafter, pending
formal discharge. The personnel of
the local county board is Oscar Shep
pard. West Alexandria, chairman; Dr.
D. W. McQueen. Camden, medical
! member; R. H. Gilmore and W. A.
Marsh, Eaton, secretary and chief
clerk respectively. The chairman and
secretary have served continuously
since inception of the board. Clerk
Marsh has been with the board since
last fall, succeeding Abner Johnson,
of Eaton, following his induction into
the military service last summer. Dr.
McQueen has been on the board since
last fall, succeeding Dr. J. C. Ryder,
who resigned to go west to recuperate
from nn operation. Chairman Shep
pard served in the Civil war. He had
the distinction of being the only Civil
war veteran serving on a local board
as chairman in the state, save with
one exception, it is said.

Washington township will meet in
convention here next Tuesday after
noon and evening at the Presbyterian

In a suit filed by Leroy Beasley and
Edna Ayers against James Beasley
and others, in which the plaintiffs
sought to have a deed set aside. Judge
G. W. Mannix, of Greenville, has
handed down a decision in which he
sustains the contention of the plain
tiffs and declares the deed void, set
aside and held for naught. The deed
in question was executed by the mo
ther of the plaintiffs and conveyed to
their father, James Beasley, her half
of sixty-three acres of land in Harri
son township, the father owning the
other half. The plaintiffs averred
their mother executed the deed during
her last illness, and twenty days, be
fore her death, and was not capable
of knowing what she was doing. They
allege undue influence and fraud were
resorted to in procuring the deed.
There are two minor heirs who are
benefited by the court's decision,
which was received here Friday morn
ing. Judge Mannix presided at the
trial of the case here some days ago.
Following arguing of a motion for
a new trial in the suit of Elmer Wright
against the Indianapolis Glove com
pany, Judge G. W. Mannix, of Green
ville, granted S. B. Foos, counsel for
the plaintiff, fifteen days in which to
file an affidavit in support of his claim
that he had discovered a source of
evidence valuable to his client since
trial of the case. Wright sued the
company for $295, which he claimed
for extra labor at the local plant of
the company. The jury hearing the
case rendered a verdict in favor of
the company.
KEEPING COTTER PINS.
A very convenient method of keep
ing cotter pins of assorted sizes is to
string them through the eyes on a bit
of string or wire. In this way when
the whole lot is picked up, it is easy
to select the one needed, whereas in
fishing through a box of loose pins
there is much chance for lost temper.
