The POOL of
FLAME
by LOUIS JOSEPH VANCE
iiujsnxnoms^wr^
ILUWOyikWWC^
WQERWER
“Well soon find out." The English
man bent over the man, who was now
very quiet, but. by the constant flick
er o t his cunning eyes, still conscious.
▲ hasty examination told the Investi
gator all he needed to know about the
nature of the wound. “He'll not last
long," said Lieutenant Couch, and be
gan to converse with the local ver
nacular of Pldgin-Engllsh, about one
word in ten of which was Intelligible
to O'Rourke. As he continued to speak
the coolie’s scowl darkened and he In
terrupted with a negative motion of
his bead. The sub chief repeated his
remarks with emphasis. For reply he
got a monosyllable that sounded, as
much as anything else, like an oath.
Couch looked up. “He says he wants
water, and I suspect he won’t speak
until he gets It. Can you —V
O'Rourke fetched the half-empty
carafe and Couch put It to the coolie’s
lips, permitting him to drink as much
as he liked. But as soon as the bottle
was removed the fellow shut his
mouth like a trap and refused a word
in answer to the lieutenant’s demands
and persuasions.
“Stubborn brute," growled Couch.
“Most of these animals here belong tc
some devllsb tong or other, and they’d
rather die than say anything touchlns
on the business of the society or af
feating the Interests of a brother
member. But I think I know away to
bring him to reason. Hand me that
knife, please."
Wondering, O'Rourke tendered him
the weapon that bad brought death to
Bypher. The lieutenant wiped it cal
lously on a corner of the coolie’s •
blouse and held the keen shining
blade before his eyes, accompanying
the action with a few emphatic
phrases. A curious expression, com
pounded of sullen fury and abject pan
ic fright, showed In the Chinaman’s
eyes, and his lips were as If by magic
unsealed. However reluctant, he be
gan to chatter and spoke at length,
delivering himself of a long state
ment which Couch punctured now and
with pertinent, leading ques
tions.
At length, throwing aside the knife,
he Jumped up, strong excitement burn
ing In his eyes. “I’ve got enough from
him,** he said rapidly. *TU explain
later. You’ll help—of course; your
wife’s Involved as well as Miss Pyn
sent. But I don’t think you need fear;
we’ll be in time. Are you readyT
. . Half a minute; I've got to use
that telephone."
He ran out Into the hall, rang up
and shouted a number Into the re
ceiver. and for a few moments spoke
rapidly In a Burmese dialect.
O'Rourke gathered that he was speak
ing with a native subordinate at the
police headquarters In Rangoon.
Couch swung back Into the study.
“Qot those revolvers, slrT Then come
along; we’ll have to run for It. For
tunately our launch Is handy; other
wise . . .*’
He sprang across the veranda and
down to the lawn, O’Rourke pelting
after him.
CHAPTER XXXIV.
A night of velvet blackness, softly
opaque, lay upon land and water. The
police launch, shuddering with the vi
brations of a motor running at high
tension, sped down the silent reaches
of Rangoon River like a hunted ghost.
She ran without lights, these having
been extinguished by Couch’s direc
tions, regardless of harbor regulations
or danger. Happily the hour was late
enough to relieve them of much fear
of trouble with other craft; the upper
reaches of the river were practically
deserted.
In the bow Couch was handling the
wheel with the nonchalance of one
- from whom the river had no secretß
by night or day. To O’Rourke It seemed
no light task to pilot so slight a craft
at such high speed through that
Stygian darkness; yet the sub-chler
was accomplishing the feat without a
dlscernable trace of fear or tremor
of uncertainty.
O’Rourke sat beside him. In the
stern a police orderly acted as me
chanic, attending to the motor. These
three, no more, made up the rescue
party.
Though devoured by Impatience and
anxiety, O’Rourke forbore to question
Couch, hesitating to divert his atten
tion from his task and knowing that
as soon as he could the young lieu
tenant would speak. From the time
when the coolie had yielded, theie
had been not a second’s rest for eith
er; neither had had time to confer
save on Questions of the most lmme*
dlate moment; and control of these
Couch bad voluntarily and naturally
assumed, deciding, acting and direct
ing In the same thought, apparently.
: “Your wife, with - Miss Pynsent,"
said Couch abruptly, without looking
j round —“at least I presume it’s Mrs.
O'Rourke, from what you say—have
been kidnaped by a gang of highbind
’ era and are now aboard a junk in the
lower river, which will sail for God
' knows-wbere at the turn of the tide,
j That’s the only thing that saves ’em.
Weil be on ’em before they’re able to
! force away down the river.”
O’Rourke groaned, holding his head
, with both hands. "My wife . . .!”
he said brokenly.
“I know,” Couch interrupted grim
ly; “I know how you feel. Miss Pyn
r.ent Is there, too, you see.”
"Oh,” said O'Rourke, “I didn't un
derstand that ... I’m sorry." He
dropped a hand on the younger man’s
shoulder and let It rest there briefly.
“Please God," he said reverently.
1 “thereil be many another polluted
yellow soul yammering at the gateo
of hell this night!"
“Amen!” said Couch. . . .We
sba’n’t be long now."
Silently O’Rourke removed his coat
and waist-coat, his collar and lawn
tie. and turned back his cuffs. “Even
ing clothes are hardly the thing to
light in,” he saiJ; "but I’m thinking
; 'twon’t make a deal of difference to
me. Got any cartridges for a Webiey
1 mark IV?"
“Wheeler has. Give Colonel
O’Rourke a few, Wheeler,’’ said Couch,
, addressing the orderly.
The latter rummaged In a locker
and pressed Into O’Rourke’s hand half
» dozen cartridges, with which the
Adventurer proceeded to replenish the
empty chamber In his revolver.
*Td only discharged one," he ob
served, “but ’tls likely we’ll need that,
even, with only the three of us
against ft Junk-load."
“Oh, I telephoned for reinforce
ment*, of course,’’ returned Couch.
“They ought to be there ahead of
ns."
"What did the coolie tell ye. If ye’ve
Ume to talk?”
Couch laughed. “I daresay you’re
wondering how I made him speak at
all."
"That’s the true word for ye."
“I threatened to cut off his silly pig
tail and send him naked and dishonor
ed to the ghostly halls of his ances
tors. It’s wonderful how much those
callous brutes dote on that decora
tion. 1 told him further, that If he
lied, when I found It out I’d return
and ehave him bald as an egg. even if
he were dead by that time. So I per
suaded the truth from him, the whole
etory—from his side of It."
"I’m listening. . .
"He confessed he was in the-pay—
like these chaps we’re after now—of
a highly respectable Chinese merchant
and bead of one of the tongs—one of
the richest men In Rangoon, who, It
seems, was also after that ruby. I
can’t Imagine what he wanted of It,
but that'll come out, probably; the
man’s rich enough to buy dozens of
stones as fine. However ... 1
gather he’d laid his plan far ahead.
The coolies intimated you’d been
watched all the way from Bombay. At
all events, the brutes were ready
when you arrived; Sypher was a
doomed man from the moment you
handed over the Pool of Flame. They
surrounded his house this night, com
ing up from the river, just as soon as
It was dark enough to conceal their
actions. Then they found a third ele
ment in the business —your friend Des
Trebes, all unsuspicious of them, lurk
ing on the veranda and watching Sy
pher through the window. So they
waited to see what he was up to. And
pretty soon they found out. Sypher
came downstairs, went to the safe anij
opened It; I presume he had the stone
In his hand, ready to put away. While
he was standing there the Frenchman
slipped up behind and stabbed
him, annexing the stone and leav
ing the way he got In. The in
stant he stepped off the veranda the
Chinese got him; but he managed to
pcream before they could silence him
and drew the attention of the house
hold, Miss Pynsent, your wife and the
servants. So to cover things up they
had to gather them all In. The serv
ants were killed —there were three of
them —and the women . . .*’
Neither man Bpoke for a time. Then
Couch resumed.
“This coolie was an outsider —a
servant of the merchant’s —not one
of the junk gang; so he stayed ashore,
and thought it would be a fine young
ing on his own I’ve telephon
ed the bead office to arrest that
cursed merchant and confiscate bis
house and g';ods and detain anybody
they could catch connected with him.
The net’s well enough laid, and 1
think . . .”
The lights of the city became vis
ible. strung along the right bank of
the river as the launch rounded a
bend. Couch swung the little boat
out into midstream. “Half-speed,
Wheeler,” he said, adding to O’Rourke;
*T’ve got to pick out that junk. I pre
sume the right one will have all sail
set and be moving downstream with
the tide; It’s Just on the turn now
and fortunately there’s no wind worth
mentioning. ... I wish I could
see something of the other launch. 1 *
He peered anxiously into the
Ity ahead. "If there were only star
light—!” he complained bitterly.
“Stand by, Wheeler, to stop the motor.
We’ll drop alongside with the current,
as quietly as we can. Colonel
O’Rourke, will you get forward and
take the boathook and beadwarp.
please; I’m needed at the wheel and
Wheeler at the engine until we make
fast.”
Cautiously the Irishman rose, took
the boathook Couch offered him, and
crept out upon the narrow* triangle of
deck at the bows. Crouching mere,
be found the beadwarp and waited,
tense with anxious expectancy, star
ing ahead In futile effort to penetrate
The Bearding Party Btood at Bay.
the wide, shadowy reaches of the riv
er. But the mystical distances con
fused and eluded him. The launch
seemed to move, panting. In an abyss
of night. She made little noise: a
hiss of water beneath her stem; the
steady humming of the motor, throt
tled down to half speed; the muffled
gasping of the exhaust. And present
ly even these ceased at a word from
Couch, and the launch moved only
with the tide.
Abruptly a towering wall of opaque
black rose out of the darkness to
starboard. O’Rourke braced himself
.’or the Imminent instant of action,
poised so lightly upon his toes and
ingertips that a swell from a moving
vessel would have thrown him off
Us balance, perhaps overboard. The
aunch closed swiftly and silently In
ipon the black wall; it towered over
aim like a cliff; far above, he could
jee dim divisions between black and
tlack that must be the rail. And he
ihook his head, dismayed; he could
lever scale that, be thought; not even
he O’Rourke could accomplish a mir
tcle. But in a breath !t had faded
>ack, and he realized that the tower
ng poop of the junk had misled him.
They were now alongside at the waist,
.-ie stood up and saw a low railing
noving and caught it over the edge of
the rail, drew the launch In, let go the
boathook and, with the beadwarp
wrapped about his hand. Jumped
blindly.
Something dealt him a vicious, all
but paralysing, blow In the pit of the
stomach; he doubled up, for a mo
ment helpless, across the Junk’s rail,
but retained sufficient presence of
mind to hold on to the headwarp.
Then, recovering a trifle, he squirmed
over and fell sprawling upon the deck,
his heels drumming an abrupt and
violent alarm. From somewhere he
heard a shrill jabbering arise, with an
ensuing patter of bare feet. Swiftly
he got upon his knees and drew in the
headwarp, with his free hand search
ing along the rail for a cleat. Some
thing thumped heavily on the deck be
side him, and grunted; and something
else followed with a second bump;
and the launch swung outward and,
caught by the current, jerked the
headwarp from his grasp. “May the
luck of the O’Rourke still hold!” he
prayed fervently, getting upon his feet
to realize that, with Couch and the
man Wheeler, he was imprisoned
aboard the junk, doomed there to re
main whatever might befall, until
the coming of the second launch . .
. or perhaps for a longer time.
As he rose some indistinct body ran
into him and cannoned off with an un
couth yelp; with no time to draw his
revolvers, the adventurer struck out
i with a bare hand and had the satis
-1 faction of finding a goal for bis blow
—of landing heavily on bare flash and
or nearing me aun sound of a fail
upon the deck.
Synchronously lights were flashing
out for and aft. A revolver spat ven
omously beside him. Somewhere a
man screamed and fell, whimpering
horribly. The revolver expoded a sec
ond time. There were confused
noises, as of a furious struggle, rough
and tumble, and he suspected that one
or another of his companions had
been tackled bodily by one of the
junk’s crew. On his own part he
caught a glimpse of a shadow moving
ghostlike against one of the lights,
and promptly exorcised It with a shot.
By this time the vessel seemed to
be caught in the grip of pandemon
ium; shouts and shots vied with
screams, groans, confused padding
footsteps, to make the moment one of
a nightmare. The boarding party stood
at bay, not daring to venture from the
spot on which they had landed, firing
steadily but with discretion.
Huddled together like children In
fear of the powers of darkness, the
three held their fire against the Inev
itable assault in force, handicapped
fearfully by their absolute ignorance
of the lay of the deck, of the number
of their opponents, and of from which
quarter they had to expect the attack.
And the silence and the suspense
wore upon their nerves until the final
struggle came in the shape of a boom
to save them from madness. And It
came with a rush and a will, cyclonic,
tremendous, overpowering. By sheer
weight of human flesh the Europeans
were pinned against the rail, fighting
at handgrips with a ciuel and cunning
foe far better prepared for such busi
ness than they. For at such close
quarters pistols were practically
worthless save as clubs, while knives
could slip to slay through almost any
interstice, however straitened.
O’Rourke had no time to think of his
companions. Stung to desperation by
the silent, unrelenting fury of his as
sailants —twice he was conscious of
the white-hot agony of a knife-thrust,
one penetrating the flesh of his side
and scraping his ribs, the other biting
deep into his thigh—he fired until he
had but one cartridge left In his re
volver, and expended that blowing out
the brains of an extraordinarily per
sistent coolie, then dropped the use
less weapon and trusted to his naked
strength.
It served him well for a little. One
man. precipitated by the weight of
those behind him Into the adventur
er’s arms, he seised by the throat and
throttled In a twinkling; then lifting
him from the deck, he exerted hie
power to the utmost, and cast the
body like a log into the midst of the
melee. Thus clearing a little space, he
found himself able to step aside and
let another run past him Into the bul
wark; and seeing the sheen of a
sword blade la the fellow’s hand, be
fore he could rseover seized his wrist,
twisted It savagely, and wrenched the
weapon away.
The finale oama a moment later, sig
nalized by a blinding flash of light
more bright than that of day. which
fell athwart the deck and Illuminated
instantaneously every inch of the
fighting ground. Fervently be blessed
the near by veesel that had turned its
searchlight on the Junk. The scene
it revealed beggared the experience of
a man whose trade was fighting; it
fell upon decks slippery with blood
and littered with the bodies of dead
and w’ounded; it silenced a confusion
ndescrlbable. Upon that Insaneturmoil
the light fell with the effect of a thun
derbolt from a clear sky. |
Screaming shrilly In their panic, the
r «e inese scattered and fell away, leav
ing O’Rourke beside Couch. Wheeler
being down and buried beneath three
Chinese corpses. And instantaneously
something grated harshly against the
starboard side of the Junk, and a man
his figure stark black against the co!d
white glare, leaped upon the rail and
tumbled inboard. Others to the num
ber of a dozen followed him. swarm
ing over the decks. Couch reeled to
wards them, babbling orders and in
structions.
The second launch had arrived.
Sick and faint, O’Rourke slouched
back against the rail, watching with
lack-luster eyes the end of the chap
ter. It was simple to the point of
seeming farcical In comparison with
that which preceded it The dazed and
now outnumbered Chinese offered no
further resistance. Disarmed and put
under guard, they disappeared from
his consciousness, while he watched
the men from the second launch,
spurred by Couch, scatter in search of
the abducted women.
Loss of blood was beginning to tell
upon him; his strength seemed alto
gether gone; his wits buzzed in his
head like a swarm of gnats. He
grasped his support convulsively, be
ginning to appreciate how seriously
he was hurt. He heard as from a great
distance thin, faint cries of men shout
ing in triumph; saw Couch, a pygmy
shape, holding in his arms a doll who
wore the face of Miss Pynsent. Then
of a sudden he was conscious of a
woman hastening toward him, a fan
tastic and incongruous figure in a din
ner-gown, her skirts trailing in the
slime of the shambles, her arms out
held to him; and knew her for his
wife. 1
The Woman Gasped Faintly and
Clung Tightly to Her Husband’s
Arm.
tie ensttjcu lU Bptafc, uui tuuiu uu>.
He felt her arms close about him. In
the face of the searchlight’s penetrat
ing and undeviating glare, night
closed down upon him.
CHAPTER XXXV.
In after days, when he was alto
gether well and whole, they Journeyed
forth, these two, the man and his
wife, from Rangoon northward. The
railway carried them some distance;
later they struck off with their train
into the primitive wilderness beyond
the ultimate British outposts on the
Chlndwine, main tributary to the Ir
rawaddy.
The land was peaceful, hospitable,
and very, very lovery in Its wilder
ness. Tbelr happiness was ecstasy.
By day they rode through Jungle,
wood and rolling uplands, or less easi
ly through the fastnesses of the hills,
side by side, thought linked to
thought, their hearts attuned. By
night their camps were pitched In a
new-found world of beauty, wonderful
in its shadowy mystery.
It was so ordered that they came,
toward sundown of n certain day, to
the foot of a hill crowned with a great
pagoda of many multiplied roofs fring
ed with a myriad silver bells that
tinkled ceaselessly in the evening
airs.
Here they dismounted and together
made the ascent of an age-old wooden
stairway, broad and easy, and throng
ed from the first rise to the last with
weary pilgrims, beggars, lepers, laugh
ing children, mendicant holy men. The
sun was low upon the horizon when,
having bribed their way along that
gauntlet, O’Rourke and hla bride (she
could never be aught less to him) at
tained to the topmost platform and.
having received permission, with meet
show of reverenoe entered the temple.
It was very dark inside and for a
time they moved bllddly In and out;
but at length they came to a massive
doorway looking toward the West, tad
here they paused, hand In hand, look
ing up to the placid face of a huge
Buddha, who squatting cross-legged
upon a pedestal, looked through the
Incense-scented gloom ceaselessly for
ward to Nirvana.
The figure, carven originally from
stone, had been so heavily plastered
with gold-leaves by the devout, that
now It had all the semblance of being
gold to its core; and, lavishly deco
rated with necklaces and bracelets of
rare jewels set In crusted gold, In the
evening glow it shone like some great
lamp of holiness. Only Its face was
In shadow.
Slowly the light struck higher be
neath the eaves of the pagoda, and
slowly It crept up and yet up, until Its
last blood-red shaft revealed the Bud
dha’s forehead and what wag set
therein, a monstrous ruby.
The woman gasped faintly and
clung tightly to her husband’s arm.
He held her close, watching the great
stone flame and throb and pulse, like
i pool of living flame swimming In
larkness.
And then the light of the world
went out
Pensively In the dusk they descend
ed the temple staircase. At the foot,
jefore they remounted their horses,
he woman came to the man and put
ler hands upon his shoulders.
“Terence,” she said. “I think I am
rery weary. Take me home."
He gathered her into his arms.
"I think," she said. "It frightened
ne—made me fearful of this country
—the Pool of Flame, up there.”
• "Ye’ve seen the last of It,” he said
enderly, “and so have I. 'Tis done
vlth, like the days of me adventur
ngs. I have no thought but you, dear
iearL Let us go home.”
THE END.
Bee's Eye for Landmarks.
There can be little doubt that so in
telligent an insect as a bee has a long
memory and a keen eye for land
marks. This Is testified by the ract
that almost all bees fly by day, where
as such creatures as moths—of no
fixed home or occupation—fly in the
darkness. A bee would be lost at
midnight because It could not recog
nize Its surroundings.

SHASTA COURIER
Chiuese Suffrage.
The opposition in U.is State are just now
making a great outcry about the danger that
the Chinese will be allowed to vote, which
they would fain persuade their dupes is one
of the objects of the Union party. No one
knows better than these very men that there
is no danger of anything of the kind. The
Chinese cannot become citizens; they do
not wish to become citizens, and there is no
desire on the part of any one to permit them
to hecomd citizens.
All this outcry of the Democrats about
Chinese suffrage and coolie labor is merely
for the purpose of catching votes. If the
Chinese could be relied i pon to support the
Copperhead ticket, the Democratic leaders
would want them all naturalized to-morrow ;
it there was tiny possibility of in king slaves
of them, the very nun who now have so
much to say of the evils of cheap labor,
would be the warmest advocates of coolie
immigration. This is no mere partisan
statement, but is borne out by facts. At this
very time the Democrats of the South are
importing coolies from Havana, and if they
were permitted would sunn stock every
plantation with Mongolian slaves. Nor are
the Democrats of this State one whit Letter.
In 1852 that consistent Democrat, Mr.
Peachy, of this city, introduced a bill in the
Legislature, known as the “ Coolie hill,”
which was designed to encourage the intro
duction and permit tho holding of Chinese
slates, by legalizing labor contracts made in
China, and providing for their enforcement
in tins State. This Coolie bill was defeated
by the opposition of men who are now mem
bers of the Union party, and are charged by
those who then advocated it with desiring
the introduction of coolie labor and the ad
mission of the Chinese to citizenship.—
Janies W. Coffroth, the Democratic candi
date for Congress in the Second District,
has now a good deal to say on the Coolie
question ; he doesn’t want the Chinese to
vote ; he doesn’t want them to come here ;
he doesn’t want their labor to be brought
into competition with free white labor. All
these things he charges on the Black Re
publicans” in true Democratic fashion. Yet
this same James W. Coffroth wasoncof the
Democratic members of the Legislature who,
in 1852. voted fur the Coolie bill introduced
by the Democrat Peachy and supported as
a Democratic measure. That bill was vir
tually to establish slavery in its worst form
in California, and if it had become law our
labor system would to day have been what
that of Cuba and the French tropical pos
sessions is at present. The coolies brought
here under the bill would be nominally
laborers bound by contract to work for years
for a dollar or two a month ; but in reality
they would be slaves, as the Chinese appren
tiees in the West Indies are. They would
be bought, sold, worked and treated like
slaves. The profit which their labor would
have yielded in onr rich placers would have
induced capitalists to bring them over bv
thousands ; the poor whites would have been
driven to the wall—compelled to leave the
State. In place of our present hardv. in
dustrious, independent, white population,
we would have had a class of rich nabobs
controlling all tbe capital and all the power
in the Stale, their poor white dependents
and their thousands of Mongolian chattels,
working under the lash.
These Copperhead demagogues talk of
prohibiting the introduction of Chinese, but
nothing can be further from their intention.
When they had power did they do anything
of ttie kind ? They controlled the State and
nation for years; did they prohibit the in
troduction of Chinese? Nor would they do
so if they were placed in power again ? Lei
there be a Democratic majority in Congress,
and its very first act would be to provide a
way for tbe re establishment i f slavery in
the South, by the passage of a national
Coolie bill,” like that they tried to pass in
this State in 1852. This is what tbe planters
of the South most ardently desiro ; even now
they are striving to replace their negro
slaves by coolies, and we know that their will
is tbe law of the Democratic party.
These Democratic orators and writers
should be the last men in the world to prate
of their attachment to the interests of labor
and of their desire to preserve the white
laborer from degrading competition. Their
whole object has been to degrade labor by
preserving and extending slavery; to bring
the poor white laborer into the most degrading
competition possible—the competition with
slavery. In despite of their best efforts
slavery has been crushed ; but give them
another lease of power and. if they do not
re enslave the freed men of the South, thev
will quickly bring them to the worse condi
tion of peonage, and re open a worse slave
trade under the guise of a contract system.
Democrats prevent the further introduction
of coolie* 1 That is just what the Democrats
of the South want; and if their party gets
into power they will have it; nor will the
rich Democrats' of this State be behind in
availing themselves of the opportunities a
national “ coolie bill” will give them, but
will quickly flood California with their slaves.
—S. F. Jimes.
A . Little Hero. —When the steamer Wis
consin was burning on Lake Ontario, one of
the passengers—Mrs. Richards, of Man-
Chester, N. If. was left with an infant on
board. The cabin boy. Warreo Tracy, of
Ogdensburg. offered to jump overboard and
swim ashoie whh the infant. The boy being
peifectly cool mother reposed confidence
in him and handed him the infant. lie
immediately jumped into the water, and,
notwithstanding he was partially stunned hv
eome one who jumped upon him from the
wreck, he reached the shore safelv with his
charge. The joy of the mother—who was
the last but one to leave the steamer, can be
better imagined than described.

Geuerai Lougstreet’s Letter.
There is more truth in’the following ex
tract from a letter recently written by Gen
eral Longstreet. that can he found in hun
dreds of verbose newspaper columns that
have been written upon the subject which he
so ably bandies. General Longstreet says :
Like other Southern men, I naturally
sought alliance with the Democratic party,
merely because it was opposed to the Re
publican party. But as far as I can judge,
there is nothing tangible about it, except the
issues that were staked upon the war, and
there lost. Finding nothing to take hold of
except prejudi e. which cannot be worked
into good fir any one, it is proper and right
that I should seek some stand-point from
which good may be done.
If I appreciate the principles of the Dem
ocratic party, its prominent features oppose
the enfranchisement of the colored man, and
deny the right to legislate upon the subject
of suffrage except by the States indie dually.
These two features have a tendency to ex
clude Southern men from that party, for the
colored man is already enfranchised here,
and we cannot seek alliance with a party
that would restrict his rights. The exclu
sive right of the States to legislate upon
suffrage will make the enfranchisement of
the blacks, whether for better or for worse, a
fixture among us. It appears, therefore,
that those who cry loudest against this new
order of things as a public calamity, are
those whose principles would fix it upon us
without a remedy. Hence it becomes us to
insist that suffrage should be extended in all
the States, and fully tested. The people of
the North should adopt what they have
forced upon us, and if it should be proved to
be a mistake, they should remove it by the
remedy under republican principles of uni
form laws upon suffrage. If every man in
the country will meet the crisis with a proper
appreciation of our condition, and come
fairly up to bis responsibilities, on to morrow
the sun will shine with a happy people, our
fields will again begin to yield their increase,
our railroads and rivers will teem with
abundant commerce, our towns and cities
will resound with the tumult of trade, and
we shall be invigorated by the blessing of
Almighty God.
He says, in substance, that ho is done
with dead issues; that the questions of
slavery and suffrage are settled ; that the
N’orth mu-t amend laws in conflict with the
settled policy and past action of the General
Government, and then the United Stans,
like a corporate body of individuals, will
labor together for a common interest.
An Abyssinian Widower. —The Emperor
of Ahyssina is a widower, and his name is
Theodore. He is, according to the belief of
his loving subjects, and tbe traditions of tbe
empire, a lineal descendant of King Solo
man and the Qoeen of Sheba. His salary is
S3OO a y ear, and he spends bis leisure hours,
after affairs of State are concluded, in
making parasols. King Theodore being a
widower, and hearing that tbe Prince Con
sort of Great Britain bad departed this life
made application for the hand of Queen
Victoria, through tbe English Consul of bis
Court. Of course the application was re
fused, whereupon the indignant King, show
ing that Abyssinia bad no fury like a Theo
dure scorned, clapped the English Consul
and several other English people, male and
female, in jail. This was three years ago.
and ever since tbe British Government bate
been trying to extricate these victims, who
meantime have been subjected to all sons cf
indignities, cruelties, privations, and even
tortures. The reason why war was not open
ly declared, was that Great Britain dreaded
the expense, and, moreover, it was well
known that at the first whisper of war, the
Consul and his fellow captives would be
slain ; while Theodore coubl retreat to tbe
interior of his kingdom, and so avoid tbe
avenging Briton. Finally. English patience
has been exnausted, and the telegraph now
informs us that a detachment of regular
troops has been ordered by the British Gov
ernment to Abyssinia to compel the King of
that country to release the British subjects
who have been held prisoners there lor a
long time past.
It may be inter;stlng to know that the
Abyssinian religion is formed on a Christian
basis, with Jewish peculiarities. The peo
ple are Caucasians of the Bedouin type,
which has been crossed with Greek, Portu
guese, Jew, Galia a - d negro. The men
are very hardy. The women have beauti
ful forms and eyes. They have a fashion of
producing ” beauty spots” on their bodies
by burning tow or cotton until a deep wound
is made. They are an immoral race, cruel,
treacherous and debauched. The language
is Giz, or a derivative therefrom.
The English will have their hands full in
this undertaking with tho jealous and an
gered descendant of Solomon, and nothing
less than tho destruction of the prisoners mav
be expected.
Good for McMillan.— Dr. McMillan, the
witness in the Surratt trial, who called one
of the counsel for the defence a “ coward
and a sneak,” will have the admiration of a
great many who have suffered from the in
snlence and impudence of lawyer? in flic
court room. Too many of our judges allow
counsel to torment witnesses, and protect
them from any retort on the part of the lat
ter. The remark of Dr. McMillan correct
ly characterized the performance, ns it is
cowardly to strike a man who is bound and
unable to resist. Lawyers in the court room
use language to witnesses which they would
not dare to employ at other times. They
abuse and-nssail witnesses when the latter
cannot reply, and almost convince tho latter
that they are criminals. The lawyer who,
under cover of bis private privileges, en
deavors to entrap bis victim into contradic
tions, or throw out insinuations against his
character which the witness has no oppor
tunity to disprove, is essentially a coward
and a sneak, and Dr. McMillan should have
the thanks of all witnesses for disregarding
etiquette and announcing the truth. The
laws of New York now, we believe, protect
the witness. The truth will more folly ap
pear when the witness is allowed to give his
testimony calmly. — Bondoul {N. I”.) Free
man.

New York Shop Girls.
A correspondent of the Watchman and
Reflector is presenting some pictures of life
among the lowly in New York, some of
which are enough to cause any friend of
humanity to weep as well as tremble, in
view of the temptations to which thousands
of the innocent but unf jrtunatc are exposed :
Last week I gave a description of the
every day life of one of tho thirty thousand
sewing girls of New York. I shall this
week present another picture—a different
phase of the same sad life—for the contem
plation of the philanthropist and the Chris
tian.
One day last winter there cnine into my
office a young girl whom I had known when
she was a little child, light hearted and hap
py, on her father’s farm in New Jersey.—
Alisfortune overtook her father, and she was
eventually left an orphan. Having an
uncle in New York, she came here to live.
They were in good circumstances, and bade
Minnie welcome; but in a few months later
the unde was lei into speculation, and lust
everything he had. In this position of
affairs Minnie sought for employment and
found it; but such employment! She came
to me, having 1 arned my whereabouts, to
solicit aid. She was now eighteen years old,
and remarkably beautiful. It was piteous to
listen to her story.
“ I get up at six o’clock,” she said, “ dress
mysell in the cold, eat a mouthful of some
thing—little enough there is to cat, and
hurry to tho shop. We are not allowed to
warm ourselves by the fire, no matter how
cold or wet we may be, and all day long I
am shivering. My work is making paper
bags, at thirty five cents a thousand.”
** And bow many can you make in a
day?”
•• One thousand, when I work hard.”
Thirty-five cents for a whole day of hard
labor, in a cold room, surrounded by girls
who are prohibited strictly from conversing
with each other, and among whom it is a
grave offence to look out of a wiisdow, or
stand a moment by a stove.
“ Tbe first day I was there,” said Minnie,
“ I felt so bad that I couldn’t keep from cry
ing ; and when I turned and spoke to the
girl who worked next to me. she stared in
affright, and made no answer. The over
seer of the room is a cross man, and ho came
and told me to go home if I wanted to talk—
they didn’t allow any gabbling there.”
I put on my overcoat and walked with
Minnie to the shop where she worked, to see
for myself. It was a long, barren room on
the fourth story of a building, with a chilly
air on it that sent a shiver through me. It
was about noon time—a little time after—
and the overseer chanced to be away.—
About a score of girls, from ten to eighteen
years of age, were seated at long tables, with
piles of brown paper and pots of paste be
fore them. Great was their astonishment
when I took my place at Minnie’s side to
watch her work, for there was a card on the
door which said, “ Positively no Admit
tance.” These faces were, without cxcep
tions, sad and sickly, and they bent over
their work with a weary air that touched me
almost to tears.
The most expert of those girls is able to
earn about three dollars a week, I was told
many earn less. None of them pay less than
three dollars a week for their board. How,
then, do they live? How clothe themselves?
Can you unriddle this puzzle? Good met!
and women are shuddering over it every
day.
It has been Minnie’s misfortune that she
was never taught to work. How could her
parents have forseen her destiny ? I sought
lung in vain to find her employment more
remunerative, in which she was capable of
engaging. While thus seeking, I heard from
her occasionally. First there came the
pleasant tidings that the cross overseer treat
ed her with especial kindness—spoke to her
cheerfully, and gave her the lightest work to
do that the place afforded.
She was so gladdened by this, in the inno
cenca of her heart! And then, not long
after, came another word ; he had grossly
insulted her, and when she repulsed him
had turned her from the shop with curses.
Does my reader understand what all this
means? I have related the incident only to
illustrate, ns modestly as may be, tbe perils
which beset the shop girl.
You will fail of realizing the ghastliness of
the subject, if you lose sight of the fact that
those who suffer thus and are tempted thus
who lead a life of friendlessness only ex
changeable for a drearier horror—of hunger
and cold, and exhaustive toil, the release of
which can be bought but with death or
shame—are a vast army in numbers. There
are thousands who want even for the misera
b!e privilege of such toil as I have tried to
picture—who perish.by the wayside, year by
year. No night passes over your head that
does not cover with its darkness somo poor
girl wandering through tho streets of New
York hungry, weary, friendless, utterly for
lorn ; who has no refuge but to give herself
into the hands of a policeman, to be taken
to the station house, that she may sleep on
its bare floor, in company with thieves,
drunkards, and shameless ones of both
sexes.
Much attention of late is being directed to
the subject of woman’s rights, in the nobler
meaning of the terra, and it is well that this
is so. The mi re fully the rising generation
of girls are taught to ho self hopeful, tho
better it will be for the future welfare of the
land—the more narrow will become the
spread of distress among the sex, as they
become widowed, orphaned, or otherwise
protectories?. Meantime, here in New York
is the existing evil of the hour which ought
not to be, must not be, overlooked. God
speed the work of all who labor in the
field 1
How Fav Cave to be a Candidate.—We
have recently been informed, says an ex
change. of the manner in which Fay became
a candidate for Governor. It seems that Fay
was President of an insurance company in
Saa Francisco, and a largo number of the
stockholders wished him to resign, which he
was not inclined to do.' To effect their ob
ject, the said stockholders procured a list of
names favoring Fay for Governor. The bait
took and Fay resigned ; the movers then
dropped the matter, and Fay fell very much
like a rotten pumpkin, and all that remains
is that which might be expected from such a
fall.

wait;
Wait a moment, young man, before you
throw that money down on the bar and de
mand a glass of brandy and water. Ask
yourself if twenty five cents cannot be better
invested in something else. Put it back iu
your pocket and give it to the little cripple
who sells matches on the corner. Take our
word for it, you will not be sorry.
Wait, madam—think twice before you
decide on that hundred dollar shawl. A
hundred dollars is a great deal of money ;
one dollar is a great deal, when people once
consider the amount of goodness it will
accomplish, in careful hands. Your hus
band’s business is uncertain ; there is a
financial crisis close at band. Who knows
what that hundred dollars may bo to you
yet.
Wait, sir, before you buy that gaudy
amethyst brest-pia you are surveying so ear
nestly through the jeweler’s plate-glass win
dows. Keep your money for another pieco
of Jewelry—a plain gold wedding ring, made
to fit a rosy finger that you wot of. A shirt,
neatly ironed, and stocking darned like
lace work, are better than gilt brooches and
flaming amethysts. You can’t afford to
marry ? You mean you can’t afford not to
marry? Wait, and think the matter over t
Wait, mother, before you speak harshly to
the little chubby rogue who has torn his
apron and soiled his white Marseilles jacket
lie is only a child, and “ Mother” is the
sweetest word in all the world to him. Nee
dle and thread and soapsuds will repair all
damages now, but if you once teach him to
shrink from his mother, and hide away his
childish faults, that damage can not bo re
paired I
W’ait, husband, before you wonder audi
bly why your wife don't get along with
family cares and houshuld duties, “ as your
mother did.” She is doing her best—and
no woman can endure her best to be slighted.
Remember the night she sat up with the
little baba that died; remember the love and
care she bestowed on you when you had
that long fit of illness 1 Do you think she is
made of cast-iron? Wait—wait in silence
and forbearance, and the light will coma
back to her eyes, the old light of the old
days.
Wait, wife, before you speak reproachfully
to your husband when he comes home late,
and weary, and “out of sorts.” He has
worked for you all day long ; he has wres
tled hand to hand, with Care, and Selfishness,
and Greed, and all the demons that follow
in the train of money making. Let homa
be another atmosphere entirely; let him feel
that there it one place in the world where ha
can find peace, and quiet, and perfect love.
Wait, bright young girls, before you arch
your pretty eyebrows, and whisper “ old
maid” as the quiet figure steals by with
silver in its hair and crow's feet round tha
eyes. It is hard enough to lose life’s glad
ness and elasticity—it is hard enough to see
you drifting away, without adding to the bit
ter cup one drop of scorn ? You do not know
what she has endured ; you never can know
until experience teaches you, so wait, before
you sneer at the Old Maid. *
Wait, before you add a billiard room to
your house, and buy the fast horses that
Black and White and ail the rest of the
fellows covet. Wait, and think whether you
can afford it—whether your outstanding
bills are paid and your liabilities fully met,
and all the chances and changes of life duly
provided for. Waij, and ask yourself how
you would like, ten years from now, to see
your fair wife struggling with poverty, your
children shabby and want stricken, and
j-ourself a miserable hanger-on round gro
ceries and one horse gambling saloons.—
You think that is impossible ; do you remem
ber what Hazael said to the seer of old ; “ Is
thy servant a dog that he should do this
thing?”
Wait, merchant, before you tell the pale
faced boy from the country “ that you can
do nothing for him.” You can do some
thing for him ; you can give him a word of
encouragement, a word of advice. There
was a time once, when you were young, and
poor, and friendless I Have you forgotten it
already ?”
Wait, blue eyed lassie ; wait a while be
fore you say “yes” to the dashing young
fellow who says he can’t live without you.—
Wait until you have ascertained “ for sure
and for certain” as the children say, that
the cigar, and the wine-bottle, and the card
table are not to be your rivals in bis heart;
a little delay won’t hurt him, whatever ha
may say—just see if it will.
And wait, my friend in the brown mous
tache ; don’t commit yourself to Laura
Matilda, until you are sure that she will bo
kind to your old mother, and gentle with
your sisters, and a true loving wife to you,
instead of a mere puppet who lives on tha
breath of fashion and excitement, and re
gards the sunny side of Broadway as second
only to Elsylnm. As a general thing,
people are in too great a hurry in this world ;
we say, wait.
A Paris correspondent of the Standard
gives the following: A French gentleman
was at the Exhibition a few days ago, when
he was suddenly tapped on the shoulder by
a detectue, and requested toaccompany him
to the office of the Commissairc de Police,
where he was charged with being a pick
pocket. The gentleman stoutly protested
his innocence, and gave his name and ad
dress; but, unfortunately, on being searched,
a number of watches, chains, and purse*
were found in his pockets. Here seemed to
be proof positive, but how came they there?
That was the question. However, fortunate
ly for the gentleman, the acute detective
managed to solve the mystery. It appeared
that he had that morning bought a new hat,
which be was assured by the hatter was the
last new English fashion, for an English
“ milord” had had a dozen of them made
expressly. Ces AnalaU sont ei iizarres. —
The detective at once guessed that this
“ milord” must be no other than the chief of
a band of pickpockets, who had these hats
made for his comrades, in order to dis
tinguish them in a crowd. “ I see it all,”
said tha detective; “one of these light-fin
gored gentry has mistaken you fur their com
rade, and being in danger, has passed,
according to their habit, these jewels and
purses into your coat pocket.” The detec
tive was right, fur on the next day the whole
of the gang was captured at the Exhibition,
the hat having afforded a clew to their det«c>
turn.

I "Words are not adequate to express the

p fion. I now refer to the system of supnly

I and Singapore, where we tlrid these brutes
—"labor agents" they style themselves—
in all of their detestable power.
"Jo the 'labor agent,' if you please, the
Borneo tobacco planter goes, saying that
be ii in need of 300 coolies. There is no
native labor in Borneo, you know, hence
tbedraft is made upon the slave deaier,
who promises the planter that the
Ben ehali be forthcoming, and that
they will cost him SIOO per man—
; this in Mexican money, which, by the
1 w wf, is in almost universal use down

tbeTe. Out of this SIOO the slave dealer
agrees to pay each coolie about $30 —which
•am the tobacco planter, if he is a 'just
and wise man.' is expected in turn to de
duct from each coolie's wage and, of
course, put into his own pocket. Thus,
you see, the poor slave is paying the first
installment on his own hire. The slave
dealer, or 'labor begins operations
,by sending his agents up into the high

NEW YORK, Dec. 12.— [Special Corre
spondence.]—"lt is harder to keep a great
fortune than it is to make one." Thus
Commodore Vanderbilt warned his son
William H. that his task would be a more
difficult one if he preserved and increased
the great wealth that he was to inherit
than was that involved in creating it.
If Commodore Vanderbilt's judgment is
true, then George J. Gould has before him
supretner tests of ability to meet than
were entailed in the creation of Jay
Gould's fortune. There may be no dram
atic battles with the great financiers; 110
startling exhibitions of strategy and dis
play of splendid audacity and. courage,
such as characterised Jay Gould*s creative
career, for conservatism depends upon
other qualities than these; but watchful
ness, caution, foresight, knowledge ot men,
of national resources, of political tenden
cies and material development, as well as
a keen, intuitive perception of the hidden
purposes of men will necessarily bo exer
cised every day if George Gould and his
brothers are to preserve what their father
made.
In one respect the position tnese young
men are now compelled to take in the
world of finance and business energy is
peculiar. They are the only inheritors of
one of those colossal fortunes made since
the close of the civil war who have re
ceived it from the creator of it and who
are expected to. do with it as he would
have done.
The Vanderbiits of the present day are
of the third generation. They are not the
sons, but the grandsons, of the maker of
the enormous property. Their father,
William 11. Vanderbilt, had the same re
lation to the Vanderbilt fortune that the
sons of Gould now bear to that which he
created. William H. Vanderbilt, when
he received his inheritance, was as
old as Jay Gould was when he died.
The second Vanderbilt had his for
tune some ten years, and so well
did he manage it that the great financiers
looked upon him as having done almost
as great things as his father, and some of
his achievements were almost as dramatic.
He preserved, not as the miser does, but
as the great capitalist should do, by devel
opment. and he gave to his sons nearly
double the fortune that the commodore
made.
William A. Vanderbilt, a man past 50
when he received bis wealth, is the only
person to whom the Gould boys can point
as having had the same experience as that
upou which they are now entering. The
Astors of this generation are the great
grandsons of the founder of the Astor
wealth. The men who since the war
have amassed colossal fortunes in
the far West either by mines or
railway development cannot expect to
maintain these possessions as the Gould
fortuue will be maintained and developed.
Senator Stanford's son is commemorated
in a great university; Mr. Huntington has
no one upon whom he can lean as Gould
did upon his son George; the Hopkins and
the Crocker millions are already scattered
to some extent; Flood and O Brien and
ex-Senator Fair and their fortunes were of
influence that is now a tradition and can
not b« renewed in their heirs, and Mr.
Mackav's son as yet gives no hint of 6uoh
pre-eminent ability as is necessary' totnake
his name as potent as his father's has been
and now is. bidnqy Dillon is a name that
is now of the past. Russell ciage has
no children, and perhaps the greatest
constructive genius of ail of the giants
developed since the war, John D. Rocke
feller, cannot hope that his achievements
will l»e suggested by anything that his
heirs may do. Therefore the sons of Jay
Gould, on the threshold of life as they
really are. are realiy the first, William 11.
Vanderbilt excepted, to show what may
be done in the way of maintaining one of
the half-dozen colossal fortunes created
since the war.
The impression among the other finan
ciers of New York s tnat George Gould
will justify his father's confidence. Cer
tainly he and his brothers are to be num
bered among the greater powers in the
financial world, and some things can now
be told of them which will furnish some
hint of their capacity to meet the enor
mous responsibilities that have now come
to them.
It !ms been said by those who know the
two older sons, George and Edwin, that
together they would mute the Jay Gould
that Wall street knew. Certainly not
physically, however. They are larger men
than their father was. George Gould is
but little taller than Jay Gould was, but
is of much stouter figure, suggesting far
greater physical strength. He is esteemed
1 a handsome young man, for kis features
are regular, his motions and habits those
of grace, and his expfeasion pltytsing. He
possesses that rich olive completion which
is associated with those born in semi-trop
ical climates, and his eyes are a* black and
at times quite as wonderful in iteir glances
as were his father's.
Edwin Gould is tall, perhaps five feet
ten, of more angular figure lighter
complexioned than his brother;-whom be
resembles neither in figure not manner
isms. Each is fond of fresh air,;of athletic
sports, George of a yacht, s£dwin of
spirited horses, fencing and hurdle riding.
The habits of the young been
correct since they went out world.
They cannot be trapped, their in
heritance cannot be threatened "by any of

since both these young men, they

ana by; Edwin delights in tty cavalry

mer rode in Troop A through t~e streets

Neither is what is called a club man. and
their social intimacies are not with the

Thus, in a few words, the personal and
social characteristics of these young men
may be set forth. They are of the kind

that serves the business of maintaining

Responsibilities were placed gradually

Trifling tests were followed by greater

Last evening quite a party came by team

icism of our performance, and so sub

anything we state at all, that we carry

pectant faces, to the opera house. Their

pect that the "attraction" is going to
place himself in all these attitudes.

'Tiay something, Madeline." again

And so I watch sadly from across the

The Sepulture of Black Bird, War Chtaf
of the Omaha*--Canoe and Scaffold
If it be true that we originally sprang
from trees, and that like those human
esque verd-antiques there is as much of
the human race under ground as above it,
then the pine tree ia the true ambush out
of which the shade of the sabled Indian
sprang and back to whose closed tent he
retires when bis tent on earth is struck.
The totems, the watons, the Masonry
of the medicine bag are all inclosed in
its burial urn and the Druidical
legends of aboriginal destiny sealed in the
chronicles of its Apocryphal hooks. The
pine tree is the true Lndian funeral tree.
It keeps the ghost of the dead fed at its
granary and the memory of the departed
green in the tropics of its eternal youth. It
mellows the sun in its midday twilight to
a moon of days, and, bowed with snow,
holds in its mysterious spell the leafy shut
of summer. It is the tilulant tree.
What a weird reflection of sounds plays
in its muffled harp!—the low* sigh as tha
dream breath of the zephyr stirs in it; the
pathetic moan as the swell of the breeaa
phrases its rising and falling cadences;
the long drawn wail of the wind as it
winds down the echoing canyon ; the howl
of the red savage as the fury of the blast
smites its.wry head and seams its twisted
trunk! It is furtive, stealthy, secluded.
It listens, it points with tasselled finger
tips to the wizardry of its black sandals on
the snowy turf. There is a whisper, a cry,
a step in its strewn shadows. It is tho
silhouette of old night. The dusk face
came out of its dark. I!ut there is a burial
trait of the pine tree which has been
strangely overlooked, even by deudrolog
ical writers. It is the secret process of em
balming, particularly in a dry. rainless
air, which, preserving a corpse, makes it a
true mummy cave. To this melancholy
tree the Navajo mother goes on only one
errand —to bury a still-born chdd.
The twilight of a winter day tinged the
deep ravine as I rode down the steep trail
along the dizzy precipice into the canyon
below. I was superbly mounted on a
lithe, trained horse, who, pricking his ears
and putting his nose toward the ground,
smelled out the dim trail in the evening of
the forest like a keen pointer. A little be
hind me rode the galiant yonng guide.
Hunt, fully armed and fearless alike by
day or night in the wide detour we were
making through the Indian country. Sud
denly my horse stopped, pricked forward
his fine ears and sniffed suspiciously.
"He scents something," said the guide.
I was about to give him his head and bid
him goon, when, raising my eyes a little
on seeing the sagacious animal , lift hie
muzzle and sniff the air again, I saw a dark,
queer-looking object stretched from limb
to limb abont halt way up a pine tree that
stood just before m*.
"What is that?" *1 asked the guide,
pointing to it at the same time with
my whip.
The guide, following the direction of
my hand, looked up at the tree and ax
claimed :
"That is an Indian grave. See, it is tha
I grave of a child. The? bar? a still-born
child in a nine tree."
"Born dead," I replied; "a motto.'a
strangest sorrow."
Determined to satisfy myself thoroughly
about a thing which is considered the
inostr curious mortuary custom of any
Indian tribe, I dismounted from my
horse. who had secured for me
by his keen sense the opportunity
of seeing what I had heard about
but never found, and leaving the well
ftrained creature standing unfastened on
the trail, approached the tree. A» I did
so I saw that the object was plainly a lit
tle parileehe, blanketed and sided with
burial boards, one of which had fallen to
the ground. I also saw that the branches
of the tree leading np to the burial
case were barked near the trunk,
one above another, as if they had
been climbed by the feet of persons
'anxious to see such a rare and peculiar ob
ject of curiosity. In ascending the tree,
limb by limb, I reached, st last, the little
grave. A jealous crow called out over
head to me as I bent over it searchingly.
Sure enough, there lay the little
whitened skeleton. Some of the bones
were missing, but still enough re
mained to tell the story. The cov
ering through which it peeped out at me
was partially dragged apart, probably by
some persistent bird of prey, but the case
boards, with their holes, through which
the corda were passed that held them
toother, still pressed the tiny stranger,
whose little form had tuct the strange
mystery of death at the Tery portals of life.
Descending f rom the tree f picked up the
fallen case hoard with the cord holes iu it,
and climbing my horse renewed toy jour
ney.
—A'frrd Tennyion.
"Born dead! Born dead!" Those worde
kept ring ng in iny ears as the horse
moved slowly forward.
Scarcely had I left the sixit when a load
undulating sound, hardly lens human than
the broken sobs ot the mother herself,
called loudly over the little stretcher oat
of the top of the pine tree.
"C-o-o-o-o-o-h!"
It was certainly a siartlingly coinci
dental sound. Hal an Indian heard it.
with that stretched grave before him. ho
would have hurried from the spot, filled ag
he is with superstitions about the dead,
and catling it "had medicine." re iiled his
medicine bag with a new charm secrat.
But an Indian never revisits the grave of
a still-born chiid.
I recognized the sound instantly at tbo
familiar voice to me of the screech owl,
who, lingering round the remains of tbo
dead, broods like an ovi> spirit in such
haunted places, repeating in this instance,
however, the death knelling dirge that
would havo been uttered by tbo Navajo

LOS ANGELES. Feb. 7.— The rainstorm
has not yet extended south of Tehachapi.
SACRAMENTO, Feb. 7.— Rain began
falling heavily at 2:30 o'clock this after
noon. There was a good downpour early
this morning. •
JAMESTOWN, Feb. 7.— Light rain has
been falling throughout this section aince
morning. Advices from Strawberry, iri
the nigher Sierras, are that it rained fcr
eighteen hours up to 1 o'clock this after
noon, since which snow has been falling.
AUBURN. Feb. 7.— The present storm is
welcome to Placer County, particularly to
the mining section. The season's rainfall
to date in Auburn amounts to about 22
inches.
KESW1CK. Feb. 7.— For two days past
it has been raining continuously in Shasta
County. Creeks are swollen and the Sac
ramento at this point is rising rapidly.
STOCKTON, Feb. 7.— Rain has been
falling here since early this morning. Re
ports show that it is general throughout
the county.
MODESTO, Feb. 7.— This evening at 8
o'clock the rainfall had amounted to .24
of an inch. Cloud3 are threatening: and
the wind is from the rainy quarter. Orow
ing crops are in excellent condition in ail
hut the western portion . of Stanislaus
County.
BAKERSFIELD, Feb. 7.— Rain fell this
evenlna but only sufficient to lay the
dust.
FRESNO, Feb. 7.— Rain commenced fall
ing here at 3 o'clock this afternoon and
the downpour has been almost continuous
since then. The rain was greatly needed.
A half inch fall is estimated for this af
ternoon. •

Chinese Colons at Panama.
The ceollea will probably organize their

In almost every foreign settlement, and Is .

SECTION OF THE TONGUE OF A CAT, HIGHLY
MAGNIFIED.
Man prides himself on his achievements in the material
World, and well he may. He builds an automobile that goes
faster than any deer, a speed boat faster than any fish, an air
plane faster than an eagle. He has built the telescope that
takes his sight and intelligence billions of miles off into space.
He has invented astronomical photography, with which he
-can see “island universes” outside of our own, so far away
that it takes the light millions of years to come to us from
them, traveling 186,000 miles a second.
In addition to his telescope for FAR SEEING the genius
of man has invented the watch, the compass, and everything
that we have except the bare earth, and man has produced
the microscope with which he can see the infinitely SMALL
just as the telescope carries him trillions of miles away into
the infinitely BIG.
It is a common saying that man was put here “to eclipse
the works of nature.”
But when he considers what nature has done, this round
earth balanced in space for his home, with a sun a million
times as big as the earth to light and heat the earth, and
other planets, his pride is humbled.
And when he looks at this “photomicrograph,” which by
a combination of photography and the microscope multiplies
enormously one section of a cat’s tongue, he is inclined to
admit that “eclipsing nature” is not going to be so easy.
Everybody knows that tongues of all the “cat tribe” are
rough. The tongue of a cat might scratch the hand of an
infant, as the tongue of a lion or tiger would scrape the skin
from any part of your body, with one lick.
The photomicrograph shows what makes the cat’s tongue
rough. On a much bigger scale the same arrangement, un
doubtedly, makes the tongue of the giant cats (tigers, lions
and leopards) as rough as a blacksmith’s file for rasping down
the hoofs of horses. „
And bear in mind, in paying a tribute to nature, that this
piece of work on the cat’s tongue is coarse work compared
with other work.
On creatures so small that you could not see them, there
are parasites that live and breathe and raise their little fami
lies. They are below our power of sight, even with the
microscope, perhaps to remain outside forever beneath our
vision.
And from that world beyond the power of the microscope
there might arise an enemy that would wipe man from the
earth, as the invisible germ of the “Black Death” nearly
wiped out the people of Europe, killing one quarter of them
all. only a few centuries ago.
Man may “eclipse nature” in a small way, but he will
never cease admiring her marvelous power.
The Ferry Loan
Line a Poon to City
Washington has a very real interest in the application of
the Chesapeake Beach Railway Company for a $900,000 loan
from the Reconstruction Finance Corporation with which to
establish a ferry line extending its present facilities across
Chesapeake Bay.
The establishment of the contemplated ferry would be a
boon to the business of Washingtonians and of residents of
the Eastern Shore. It would also contribute to the pleasure
of residents of the National Capital through bringing them
closer to the resorts which abound on the Maryland
peninsula.
The proposal of the railway to establish the ferry service
has hung fire for many years.
It has been fought through the Interstate Commerce Com
mission which granted a certificate of convenience and
necessity.
It has been fought through the courts up to the United
States Supreme Court which confirmed the findings of the
rommission that the project would serve the convenience and
meet the necessity of the public.
The prolonged litigalion so delayed the start of the project
that the depression of the last few years has seriously inter
fered with its financing by private banking interests.
The Reconstruction Finance Corporation, however, has
money to lend at reasonable rates for such a project.
Granting of the loan would give work to 900 men during the
winter, according to estimates placed before the corporation.
This is the purpose of the corporation.
The principal objection to the establishment of the ferry
comes from the Claiborne-Annapolis Ferry Company, which
feels that establishment of the new line would seriously inter
fere with its patronage.
The proposed line would cut from 24 to 36 miles per trip off
the route between Washington and Eastern Shore points. It
would give service to points now only remotely touched by
existing facilities.
For these reasons it is to be doubted that the effect on the
existing ferry would be disastrous.
Certainly the new project, with the immediate employment
it would give to hundreds, deserves the serious and favorable
consideration of the Reconstruction Finance Corporation.

LISTEN, WORLD!
Everybody's Muddled ♦
It was a great relief to me
when I found that everybody's
muddled.
Os course, it was a great sur
prise at first, and, considerable
of a shock; for my elders have
■Vj
ELSIE KOHINSON
given me such
a di f f erent
idea when I
was a child!
According to
them grown
ups knew
every thing.
Gro w n ■ u p s
were never
cry babies.
Grown- ups
never made
silly mistakes.
Never dropped
and forgot
things. Never
left things
all higgledy-piggledy. Always
knew exactly what they wanted
to do. Always wanted to do
the right thing—and did it.
And if I was a good girl,
and studied hard and did what
I was told, that’s the way I'd
be some day. I, too, would be
wise, and strong, and sure, and
never make mistakes
Would I be sure, then,
whether I wanted a pink rib
bon or a blue one, without
changing my mind dozens and
dozens of times, and then wish
ing I’d gotten the other one
instead?
Those Grown-Ups
Yes, they assured me. I'd be
perfectly sure just what I
wanted all the time. And I’d
never want silly things, either,
or get mad if I couldn’t have
them. Grown-ups never wanted
silly things. Grownups never
got mad.
Naturally, with a sales talk
like that, I grew up with some
mighty glowing ideas about
adults. To be sure, they did
seem a little stuffy and stodgy
at times; and I frequently won
dered why, if they were so
smart, they didn’t have more
fun. Yet, on the whole, I had
perfect faith that they were
superior beings, forever re
moved from the frets and
furies of childhood.
As I advanced in years, came
to be the enormous age of seven
teen, this faith persisted. And
I began to look forward eagerly
to the solid years of maturity;
for seventeen is certainly a pesty
age. Page that liar who said
that youth is the happiest time
of life. I wasn’t having a happy
time. I was, generally speaking,
having the heckuva time.
Nobody understood me. Worse
yet, I didn’t understand myself.
I never knew what I wanted to
do, and, when I did, I didn’t
want to do it. Make up my
mind? How could I, when I
seemed to have a new mind
srery minute?
By Elsie Robinson
► But some day, than': goodness,
I’d emerge from all this muddle.
Some day these messy days
would be over and I. too, would
be sure and serene.
And so I went on, living and
hoping.
Still Seventeen
And presently I was 27, in
stead of 17, and should have
become a grown-up, too, with
the Guaranteed Grown-up Poise
and Power. But, to my se
cret shame and consternation,
it was nothing of the sort.
Outwardly, I put up a pretty
good bluff; but, inwardly, I
was still 17! Inwardly, I was
as far from knowing what I
wanted, or liking it when I
got it, as I had been at 7!
So the years went on, with
the accumulating conviction
that I was the world’s worst
sap. Oh, how I admired and
envied poised people! How I
wished that I, too, were “mas
ter of my fate and captain
of my soul,’’ instead of the
weak worm which I secretly
knew myself to be!
A miserable state of mind.
Doubtless you have shared it.
Yet at least 10 more years had
to pass before it began to
dawn on me that EVERY
OTHER HUMAN BEING ON
EARTH FELT THE SAME
WAY! But, when it did dawn,
I went almost ga-ga from re
lief.
All Had a Wail
“Sure and serene maturity?’’
People who knew their own
minds? My eye! There was no
such animal! In among the
rich and the great lea the
trail, but never a thoroughly
cool or competent human did
I find. I interviewed famous
philosophers, and they whined
to me about their corn plas
ters. I visited our financial
wizards, and found them fret
ting over cross-word puzzles. I
sat in the Senate gallery of
our national Capitol and
watched cry-baby exhibitions
that would have been promptly
adjourned to the woodshed
when I was 7.
And they tell me that even
Einstein can’t remember to put
on a necktie! And has been
known to pout over his oat
meal!
So there are. after all, no
grown-ups! We’re all muddled.
We’re all still trying to make
up our minds over hairpins and
mumblety-peg. And these guys
with the hard-bolied eyes and
haughty jowls are probably just
wondering if they look best in
brown.
Whoops! It’s a great discov
ery! It has taken me nearly
50 years to find it out. BUT
WATCH ME LIVE NOW!

MARRY-GO-ROUND
I By Helen Rowland
Trumps—ln the Love-Game
When it comes to marriage,
most men are a sad lot —and
most women, a sad lottery.
“The Melancholy Day”; in
spring, you buy a gay new
- ” 5 *■'
hat and plant a bright new
garden; in summer you buy a
bright new bathing-suit and
start a gay new love affair.
And, LOOK at the darned
things, now!
Getting your first husband,
like getting your first job, is
a feat. But, judging from the
ease with which a divorcee
can remarry, most men seem
to require “previous experience”
in everything, these days, from
business to matrimony.
These modern men are so
fickle,, that it makes you
dizzy’ But, what can you ex
pect of them, when a wise old
man like King Solomon had
999 changes of heart, before
he found out what kind of
girl really appealed to him.
1 THREE-MINUTE JOURNEYS
By Temple Manning "—
In Paris—the Dueling «
School Near the Paris Opera
When occasionally the cables
bring us stories of a duel
fought in France, usually on
the outskirts of Paris, there
is always renewed expression
of surprise that such things
can be in this day and age.
How much greater would be
the astonishment of people in
general if they realized that
there are many more such
duels than the few that are
recorded in the newspapers—
that there is, in fact, a duel
ing school where marksmen
still come and practice dili
gently so that they may be
feared as crack shots, and
treated with kid gloves be
cause of their reputation with
the pistol!
And that dueling school is,
of course, in Paris. Its home
is in a very fine old mansion
in the Avenue Victor Em
manuel 111, a high-class sec
tion of the city and quite near
♦ Many a man goes to his
grave, wondering whether he
captured the woman he mar
ried—or merely capitulated to

Vanity is God’s gift. No
dopbt, even a lady hippopota
mus secretly fancies that she
has “feminine charm”—and a
gentleman hippopotamus has
a secret conviction that no
girl could resist him.
Marriage may be far from
perfect. But, it IS sort of com
forting to see a man’s hat
hanging on the rack, even
though his mind or his feet
may be miles away, pursuing a
golf ball —or another woman.
Nowadays, men don’t wait for
love to fly out of the window.
They treat it so carelessly or

the opera. For more than a
century the aristocrats and
the famous have been going
to the establishment of Gas
tinne Renette to practic and
attain perfection in pistol
dueling.
The Renettes are of Norman
stock and were manufacturers
of firearms up to the time of
the French Revolution, when
one of the family went to Paris
and established the shooting
gallery, which immediately be
came the rendezvous for would
be duelists. Within the building
is ample evidence of its Old
World traditions. The business
office is very old fashioned,
and there is a leisurely air
about the place, despite its grim
purpose and its wide array of
firearms.
In the treasure room is a
marvelous exhibition of ex
quisite pistols of the type or
dained for dueling purposes.
All are muzzle loaders, for auto
matics and metallic cartridges
arc barred in dueling.
WASHINGTON, D. C.,
OCTOBER 22, 1932
When Discretion’s the
Better Part of Valor
By Claude G. Bowers
Author of “The Party Battles of the Jackson Period.” “The Struggle
For Democracy in America.” “Jefferson and Hamilton,” “The
Traffic Era.” and “Beveridge and the Progressive Era.”
WITH, all the polls indi
cating a Roosevelt
sweep, the Hoover forces,
driven to despair by the ap
proach of pay-day, are now
resorting to desperate de
vices, all of which are vicious
and some of which, in most
States, are criminal. We
have before us the names of
two concerns in Chicago that
are threatening their ein
ployes with dismissal unless
Hoover is elected. There is
just one place for employers
of this type, and that is the
penitentiary.
More vicious, because dan
gerous to the nation, is the
plan to convince the people
that the election of Mr.
Roosevelt will mean the
abandonment of the gold
standard. Secretary Hurley,
whose /dinner engagement
with the American Legion in
Colorado has been cancelled
because of his use of tanks,
bayonets and gas against a
hungry, ragged army in
Washington, has just made
that astonishing assertion.
Mr. Hoover’s speech to the
effect that in February we
were within two weeks of
going off the gold standard
until he, solitary and alone,
saved it, set the pace. That
claim has been demonstrated
to have been recklessly false
by financial writers and
bankers; it brought the mar
ket down here and the dol
lar down in Europe; and did
more to destroy confidence
than anything thus far said
or done. The Hurley state
ment is an even cheaper piece
of demagogy.
Publicly Denounced
As a Lie
It was Secretary Hurley’s
assistant in the War Depart
ment who aesorted to a
criminal libel of Mr. Roose
velt in a charge that many
years ago a firm with which
he. was associated had in
dulged in disreputable busi
ness in a stock transaction.
The attorney at the time,
announcing that he is a
Republican who will vote
for Hoover, has publicly
denounced this as a lie in a
full statement of what oc-,
curred.
Mr. Hoover has not re
buked the member of his
official household for giving
circulation to that libel.
If it is the desire of Mr.
Hoover to enter into the
private business careers of
the presidential nominees
he will open a field that is
not without its richness.
Senator Norris has just
quoted from some of Mr.
Hoover’s writings for the
Mining Magazine, of May,'
1912, to illustrate his con
cept of business ethics.
100,000 Workingmen apd
Never Had a Strike
In his speech at Cleve
land, Mr. Hoover charged
that the Democratic na
tional committee had given
it out that he had been an
importer of Chinese coolie
labor for his mines in South
Africa. And the committee
did nothing of the sort.
But in his speech the candi
date impressively pounded
the table and asserted that
no company of his had ever
imported coolie labor.
We have said in these
columns that he did employ
coolie labor, but we had the
Chinese mines in mind. Four
years ago he made the
boast that he had employed
100,000 men and never had
a strike. That was a fair
assertion, if true, in that it
would illustrate his attitude
toward labor.
It is just as fair to inquire
as to the character of this
army of workingmen. Some
years ago, when Republican
leaders were making more
damaging attacks on Mr.
Hoover than have ever been
made by his political oppo
nents, Senator Willis asked
the question: '
* ‘ What American work
ingmen did he employ?”
» And Senator Norris has
♦ just commented on that
question:
“This question so far as I
know has never been an
swered, and it seems to me
that the American people
have a right to have the
question completely an
swered.”
Assuredly it has the right
if a point is to be made that
he employed 100.000 work
ingmen and never had a
strike.
Had he not made such a
point of denying that his
firm ,had ever imported
coolie labor and coupled his
denial with a nasty attack
on Democrats, it might well
be passed in silence. But
there are documents avail
able to the public that in
dicate that his memory may
be at fault.
As early as 1898, in refer
ring to the high cost of
mining in Australia, he
wrote an article published in
the Engineering and Mining
Journal of December 17,
1898, page 426, in which he
said:
‘ ‘ The directions from
which changes should come
in order to lower the work
ing costs in the.. .removal
of restrictions on Asiatic
labor.”
The Importing
Os Chinese Coolies
This magazine article may
be read in Room 18 of the
Astor Library, in New York
city.
And Mr. Hoover’s firm did
•make a business of importing
Chinese coolies to South
Africa. He was a director of
the Chinese Engineering &
Mining Company from 1903
to 1912. In the Daily Consu
lar and Trade Reports of the
Department of Commerce
and Labor of the United
States of January 3, 1906, is
set forth a report from
American Consul General
Ragsdale, of Tientsin, on the
system that had been estab
lished with the consent of
the Chinese government for
the transportation of coolie
labor to the Transvaal. In
this official report, page
three, we read that:
"Contracts for this work
were given to two British
firms in Tientsin — William
R. Forbes & Company and
the Chinese Engineering &
Mining Company.”
Thus it appears, from the
record, that while Mr. Hoover
was a director, his company
did have the contract for
transporting coolie labor to
the Transvaal.
At a meeting of the Chi
nese Engineering & Mining
Company, September 13,
1904, the presiding officer
reported on the "advantages
offered by the port of Ching
wan-tao in connection with
! the shipping of coolies from
North China to the Trans
vaal”; and Mr. Moreing, of
the company, said :
"Although I say I was th©
originator of the enterprise,
I know that the shareholders
and directors owe a great
debt of gratitude to two men
in bringing this business to a
successful issue, namely, my
partner, Mr. Hoover, and Mr.
de Wouters of Brussels.”
This appears in the report
in the Mining Journal, Sep
tember 17, 1904, page 281.
which may be consulted at
the Astor Library.
It was at this very time
that the importation of
coolies became an acrimoni
ous subject of partisan dis
cussion in Parliament, and
Sir Henry Campbell-Banner
man, the Liberal leader, said
in debate:
"This policy was an out
rage upon the white man of
the Transvaal who, under the
sham and bastard form of
self - government that had
been extemporized, had not
been able to make their own
views known. Certainly I
said, and I shall say it again,
that in many of its features
it is indistinguishable from
slavery.”
This is found in the House
of Commons debates of
March 15, 1904.

I'nt 11 recently, science has paid little

We once labored under the supersti

Some time ago, Prof Atwater, of

I nutritive Investigation which at

; meat of chemistry of the I diversity

have developed new methods of re

The ultimate, fundamental, under
lying object of the nutrition investiga

man In order to attain the above

ond, a study of the Injurious results

leal composition, find value, digestlhll-I
Ity. economic value, methods of cook
lug meats and Influence of the con
sumption of meat upon the health. Of

The amount, diameter and value of;
'he food nutriments thus lost is an im
portant economic question White In a
private family It may not be necessary
to consider such a question, when ,
large numbers are to be fed, it will be
recognized that those meats which
shrink least In cooking, other tilings
being equal, are the most desirable
In connection with these researches
150 elaborate experiments have been
made for the purpose of determining
tin- amount, character and economic
value of the losses which occur when

common use The results of these ex
perimenta ha<e shown that the great
est loss in weight which occurs in

water In the roasting of meat the
' chief lo is due to tiie removal ot
1 both water fin I fa! Tin* losses of

of meat- are very small a- compared
; with tin* losses which lake placet in

In a large number of experiments
the average losses resulting In Ibo

found to be as follows Total loss in
weight 23.17 pounds, water 11 tiS

is quite different in (lie roasting of
